monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 1 baseline + Stage 2 annual monitoring from Year 1). Measurable chan
response_timescale: Detectable changes in microbial biomass carbon and CO₂ respi
soil_type_associations: Clay-rich soils (Vertisols, Cambisols) — Support larger microbial biomass due to higher surface area for microbial attachment and stronger aggregate microhabitat structure. Clay-associated communities are more physically protected against disturbance (Spedding et al. 2004).

Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Lower microbial biomass due to reduced surface area, lower water retention, and more rapid organic matter mineralisation. Response timescales may be shorter in sandy soils under management change.

Organic-rich soils (Histosols, Mollisols) — Support the highest microbial biomass of any soil type; carbon substrate availability is not limiting. Monitoring focuses on community composition and functional diversity rather than biomass alone.

Acidic soils (pH < 5.0) — Strongly reduced bacterial diversity due to pH-driven community collapse; acid-tolerant Acidobacteria dominate. Recovery under liming or reduced acid fertiliser inputs can be measurable within 1–2 seasons (Lauber et al. 2009). [geo]
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: CO₂ burst and Haney score increase progressively within 1–2 growing seasons of tillage cessation; bacteria-to-fungi ratio declines over 2–4 years as community shifts toward more fungal-dominated structure. P03: Soil CO₂ respiration and WEOC increase measurably within 1–2 seasons of cover crop introduction; microbial biomass carbon increases within 2–3 seasons of consistent cover cropping. P04: Haney score and CO₂ burst increase within 1–2 seasons of regular organic amendment; bacterial-to-fungal biomass ratio declines over 2–3 years as community diversifies. P05: Soil pH stabilises and trends upward within 1–3 seasons of synthetic N reduction; bacterial diversity indices (Shannon, Chao1 from metabarcoding) increase as pH stabilises over 2–4 years. P39: CO₂ burst, WEOC, and total microbial biomass carbon all increase progressively; Haney score improvement within 1–2 seasons is the primary early verification signal for this practice.
level1_protocol_name: Field CO₂ respiration assessment (jar incubation method)
level1_output_metric: CO₂ concentration after 24-hour incubation (ppm or %). Expressed as relative comparison across monitoring points and seasons — not directly comparable to laboratory-measured values. Within-programme trend assessment only.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring, when soil temperature begins rising after winter and bacterial activity accelerates. In tropical systems: early wet season. Record the same season each year for trend comparability.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn / early dry season — before soil temperature drops and bacterial activity slows. Compare same-season results across years only.
level1_equipment: 125ml glass or plastic jar with airtight lid; trowel; colorimetric CO₂ indicator tube (Dräger Polytron or equivalent — available online, ~USD 5–10 per tube) or portable CO₂ meter; digital thermometer; field datasheet and pen.
level1_reference: Field adaptation of the Haney CO₂ burst protocol (Haney et al. 2018) simplified for community observer application without laboratory equipment.
level2_protocol_name: Haney Soil Health test
level2_output_metric: Haney Soil Health score (0–100; higher = more biologically active and nutritionally balanced); CO₂ burst value (mg CO₂-C/kg dry soil); WEOC (mg/kg dry soil); WEON (mg/kg dry soil); soil pH. Report all four components — do not rely on the composite score alone.
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — when soil temperature begins rising (soil temperature above 5°C at 10cm depth [~]). This is the peak period for detecting biological activity recovery and the most reproducible window for year-on-year comparison. Record sampling date and soil temperature at time of collection.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — before soil temperature drops below 8°C [~]. Spring and autumn results together provide a within-year activity range that helps distinguish management effects from seasonal variation.
level2_equipment: Soil corer or trowel (stainless steel, cleaned between sites); collection bucket; paper sample bags; field balance (for approximate weight check); permanent marker; courier or laboratory submission system. Laboratory: standard soil laboratory with infrared CO₂ analyser and TOC analyser.
level2_reference: Haney RL, Haney EB, Smith DR, Harmel RD, White MJ (2018) The soil health tool — Theory and initial broad-scale application. Applied Soil Ecology 125:162–168.
level3_protocol_name: Phospholipid fatty acid (PLFA) microbial community analysis — bacterial fraction
level3_output_metric: Amplicon sequence variants (ASVs); alpha diversity indices (Shannon, Chao1); beta diversity (Bray-Curtis dissimilarity between sites and years); taxonomic composition at phylum and family level; functional guild prediction using PICRUSt2 or Tax4Fun.
level3_seasonal_primary: Same as Level 2 — spring primary window. Pair PLFA sampling with Haney test sampling for cost efficiency at the same field visit.
level3_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — pair with Level 2 secondary window.
level3_equipment: Specialist GC-MS laboratory; freeze-dryer; solid-phase extraction cartridges; standard GC-MS consumables. Field: same as Level 2 composite sampling.
level3_reference: Frostegård Å, Tunlid A, Bååth E (1991); Bossio DA, Scow KM (1998); Buyer JS, Sasser M (2012) — see References section.
primary_reference: Haney RL, Haney EB, Smith DR, Harmel RD, White MJ (2018) The soil health tool — Theory and initial broad-scale application. Applied Soil Ecology 125:162–168.

hard_prerequisite: None for AMF monitoring. Ectomycorrhizal monitoring (Level 2 Protocol B and Level 3 Protocol B) additionally requires tree species capable of ectomycorrhizal associations — applicable only in agroforestry, woodlot, or orchard contexts.
response_timescale: Recovery of hyphal network density and measurable changes in
soil_type_associations: High-phosphorus soils (long-term fertilised Cambisols, Luvisols) — Lowest AMF colonisation rates and spore densities — plant investment in AMF suppressed by freely available inorganic phosphorus. Most important monitoring context for detecting recovery under reduced fertiliser inputs. Colonisation baseline likely to be very low (<15% root length colonised [~]) in these soils (Treseder 2004).

Sandy low-P soils (Arenosols, Podzols) — Potentially highest AMF colonisation rates where low inorganic P availability maintains strong plant-fungal mutualism. Less monitoring need for recovery detection; more relevant for confirming AMF community stability under management change.

Acid soils (pH < 5.5) — AMF community diversity declines at very low pH — acid conditions are associated with reduced spore germination rates for many Glomeromycota taxa. Recovery of AMF communities is linked to pH recovery above 5.5 [~] (entry from Kabir 2005 and Oehl et al. 2004 context). [geo]
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Root colonisation percentage increases within 1–2 growing seasons of tillage cessation; spore density increases progressively over 2–4 years; GRSP accumulation becomes measurable over 3–5 years of consistent no-till management. P03: Root colonisation in the cash crop increases within 1–2 seasons of introducing mycorrhizal cover crops; hyphal length density measurably higher in cover-cropped versus bare-soil fallow treatments within one rotation cycle (Smith & Read 2008). P05: AMF root colonisation rate increases progressively over 2–4 years as available soil phosphorus declines; this is the slowest B2 response for this profile — P saturation in long-cultivated soils can persist for many years after fertiliser reduction and delays biological response. P39: GRSP concentrations increase measurably within 2–4 years of consistent management change; improvements in aggregate stability (as reflected by A04 abiotic precursor) are detectable 1–2 seasons before GRSP reaches detectable increase at laboratory analysis.
level1_protocol_name: Root visual assessment and aggregate stability field test
level1_output_metric: Root colonisation score (absent/sparse/moderate/abundant — per point and per site average); aggregate stability proportion (number of stable aggregates / total tested). Qualitative — within-programme trend assessment only.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring — when root growth is active and colonisation most visible in actively growing plant tissue. Avoid sampling at or after crop maturity when root senescence reduces colonisation visibility.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Early autumn — at the onset of autumn root growth following harvest or grazing.
level1_equipment: Trowel; clean water; 5 jars or cups; field datasheet; camera. No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Field adaptation of aggregate stability assessment from Rillig (2004); root colonisation visual guide adapted from Giovannetti & Mosse (1980).
level3_protocol_name: Phospholipid fatty acid (PLFA) analysis — mycorrhizal fraction (shared with Profile 01)
level3_output_metric: AMF amplicon sequence variants (ASVs); alpha diversity (Chao1 richness, Shannon diversity); community composition at genus level; beta diversity (Bray-Curtis dissimilarity between sites and years). Rhizophagus irregularis dominance is common in disturbed soils — increasing relative abundance of diverse Glomeromycota genera indicates community recovery.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — when AMF are most active in root colonisation. Pair with Protocol A PLFA sampling for cost and logistical efficiency.
level3_reference: Frostegård et al. (1991); Bossio & Scow (1998); Buyer & Sasser (2012) — see References.
level3b_protocol_name: DNA metabarcoding — 18S rRNA or ITS amplicon sequencing (AMF-specific)
primary_reference: Frostegård et al. (1991); Bossio & Scow (1998); Buyer & Sasser (2012) — see References.

monitoring_stage: Fast–medium (Stage 2 from Year 1). Functional guild composition shifts are detec
response_timescale: Bacterivore and fungivore guild ratios (Nematode Channel Rat
soil_type_associations: Sandy soils (Arenosols, Psammosols) — Lower total nematode abundance due to reduced water film continuity and lower organic substrate; community dominated by bacterivores. Plant parasites may be proportionally more numerous. Baermann funnel extraction is less effective in very dry sandy soils — use centrifugal flotation.

Clay-rich soils (Vertisols, Cambisols) — Higher total abundance and greater community diversity due to higher water retention, organic matter binding, and more complex aggregate microhabitat. More favourable for omnivore and predator guilds.

Organic-rich soils (Histosols) — Very high nematode abundance; fungivore guilds prominent due to high fungal substrate availability. Community diversity and Maturity Index typically high in undisturbed organic soils.

Waterlogged soils (Gleysols) — Reduced abundance and diversity under anaerobic conditions; community shifts toward anaerobic-tolerant bacterivore specialists. Sampling only meaningful in aerobic soil layers.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Nematode Channel Ratio shifts toward more balanced bacterial:fungal channels within 1–2 seasons; omnivore and predator guilds (cp 4–5) detectable within 2–4 years; Maturity Index increases progressively from Year 2–3 of consistent no-till. P08: Plant parasitic nematode relative abundance declines measurably within 1–3 years of rotation introduction; plant parasite index below 20% of community [~] is the target trajectory for effective rotation management. P39: Enrichment Index increases within 1–2 seasons of organic amendment addition, signalling active substrate enrichment and microbial community stimulation; this is followed by Structural Index increase over 3–5 years as higher trophic guilds recover.
level1_protocol_name: Not available
level2_protocol_name: Baermann funnel extraction and guild-level microscopic classification
level2_output_metric: Total nematode count (individuals/100g dry soil); guild proportions (% bacterivore, fungivore, plant parasite, omnivore, predator); Nematode Channel Ratio (NCR = bacterivores / (bacterivores + fungivores) — range 0–1; lower values indicate fungal-dominated, more stable system); Plant Parasite Index (PPI = plant parasites / total nematodes × 100 — % of community).
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — when soil moisture is adequate for active nematode movement and microbial activity is increasing. Avoids dry summer periods when nematodes become dormant and Baermann efficiency falls. Record soil temperature and moisture at sampling.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after first autumn rains restore soil moisture. Same-season results compared across years only.
level2_equipment: Glass Baermann funnels (one per sample); rubber tubing and clamps; fine muslin or paper tissue; compound microscope (100–200×); McMaster or Hawksley counting chamber; centrifuge (800g minimum); pipettes; field datasheet; field balance.
level2_reference: Baermann funnel protocol following Ecdysis 1000 Farms soil nematode sampling standard; guild classification following Yeates et al. (1993); NCR and PPI indices following Ferris et al. (2001).
level3_protocol_name: Centrifugal flotation extraction with full nematode faunal analysis — Maturity Index, Enrichment Index, Structural Index
level3_output_metric: Maturity Index (MI) — range 1–5; values above 2.5 [~] indicate structurally recovering food web; Enrichment Index (EI) — range 0–100; declining EI indicates transition from enriched-disturbed to stable state; Structural Index (SI) — range 0–100; increasing SI confirms higher trophic level recovery; Channel Index (CI) — declining CI indicates shift toward fungal energy channel dominance under no-till.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Level 2. Centrifugal flotation is effective across a wider moisture range than Baermann funnel.
level3_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — particularly useful for detecting plant parasite population peaks post-growing season.
level3_equipment: High-speed centrifuge (1000g minimum); 25μm sieve; 4% formalin; compound microscope (400×); identification key (Bongers 1988 or equivalent regional key); calculation spreadsheet for MI, EI, SI, CI (freely available from UC Davis Ferris lab).
level3_reference: Bongers T (1990) Maturity Index. Ferris H, Bongers T, de Goede RGM (2001) Enrichment, Structural and Channel Indices. Yeates GW et al. (1993) guild classification — see References.
primary_reference: Baermann funnel protocol following Ecdysis 1000 Farms soil nematode sampling standard; guild classification following Yeates et al. (1993); NCR and PPI indices following Ferris et al. (2001).

monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Collembola community composition responds within 1–3
response_timescale: Total Collembola abundance responds within 1–2 seasons of or
soil_type_associations: Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Lower total abundance but potentially high form diversity if litter inputs are maintained. Euedaphic forms are less common due to larger pore sizes and lower moisture retention. Flotation extraction is very effective in sandy soils.

Clay-rich soils (Vertisols, Cambisols) — Higher total abundance; euedaphic forms more abundant due to finer pore network. Cracking clay soils provide distinctive microhabitat for some euedaphic specialists. Flotation extraction requires more vigorous stirring to release individuals from soil aggregates.

Acid organic soils (Podzols, Histosols) — Very high epedaphic abundance due to thick organic horizon; distinctive acid-tolerant community with specialist genera (Megalothorax, Sminthurinus). Different reference community from neutral agricultural soils — requires separate interpretation framework. [geo]
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Total Collembola abundance increases within 1–2 seasons of tillage cessation; euedaphic forms (Onychiurus, Tullbergia, Protaphorura) detectable in the community within 2–4 years of consistent no-till; QBS-ar score increases progressively over 3–5 years. P04: Collembola total abundance increases measurably within 1–2 seasons of compost or organic amendment application; epedaphic and hemiedaphic species respond fastest; community remains at higher abundance level if organic inputs are sustained consistently. P39: Euedaphic form proportion increases progressively over 3–5 years as SOC accumulates and soil pore microhabitat recovers; form diversity (QBS-ar score) increases proportionally with SOC improvement confirmed by A01 abiotic precursor.
level1_protocol_name: Soil flotation extraction — community observer springtail count
level1_output_metric: Total Collembola count per 25cm2 × 5cm sample; proportion epedaphic vs small forms (%). Extrapolated to individuals per m2 if consistent sample area is maintained.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring and early summer — when soil moisture is adequate and microbial activity at peak sustains high abundance. Avoid sampling in very dry conditions (populations retreat deep) or immediately after heavy rain or irrigation (individuals may have moved).
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after first autumn rains restore soil moisture.
level1_equipment: Trowel; white plastic container (2 litre minimum); 1 litre clean water per sample; hand lens (10×); fine paintbrush; white card; field datasheet; camera. No specialist equipment required.
level1_reference: Simplified from the soil flotation extraction methodology described in Rusek (1998) and adapted for community observer use.
level2_protocol_name: Berlese-Tullgren funnel extraction with ecomorphological form classification
level2_output_metric: Collembola abundance (individuals per 100cm3 soil); ecomorphological form proportions (% epedaphic, hemiedaphic, euedaphic); euedaphic form ratio (euedaphic / total); QBS-ar score contribution from Collembola component — see Parisi et al. (2005) scoring table.
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — when soil moisture is adequate for active fauna. Avoid very dry or frozen soil (active fauna have retreated to depth). Record soil temperature and moisture at sampling.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after soil moisture restoration. Secondary window allows detection of seasonal community differences.
level2_equipment: Soil corer (10cm diameter) or trowel; Berlese-Tullgren funnel with 25W bulb; 4mm metal sieve; collecting vessel; 70% ethanol; stereomicroscope (10–40×); Petri dish; forceps; ecomorphological form reference guide; field datasheet; camera.
level2_reference: Berlese-Tullgren extraction following ISO 23611-2:2006 Soil quality — Sampling of soil invertebrates — Part 2: Sampling and extraction of micro-arthropods. QBS-ar index following Parisi et al. (2005).
level3_protocol_name: Species-level Collembola identification and QBS-ar index — soil quality assessment
level3_output_metric: QBS-ar score (range 0–100+; values above 80 [~] indicate structurally diverse soil arthropod community); Collembola species richness; Shannon diversity; euedaphic species richness; beta diversity between sites and years.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Level 2.
level3_equipment: Compound microscope (100–400×); Collembola identification keys (regional); QBS-ar scoring matrix (Parisi et al. 2005); dissecting needles; Hoyer's mounting medium for slide preparation; sequencing access for molecular work.
level3_reference: Hopkin SP (2007) A Key to the Collembola (Springtails) of Britain and Ireland. FSC Publications (for European fauna) [geo]; Parisi V, Menta C, Gardi C, Jacomini C, Morselli L (2005) Microarthropod communities as a tool to assess soil quality and biodiversity: a new approach in Italy. Agriculture Ecosystems and Environment 105:323–333.
primary_reference: Berlese-Tullgren extraction following ISO 23611-2:2006 Soil quality — Sampling of soil invertebrates — Part 2: Sampling and extraction of micro-arthropods. QBS-ar index following Parisi et al. (2005).

response_timescale: Total oribatid abundance and basic ecomorphological form rat
soil_type_associations: Organic-rich soils (Histosols, thick A-horizon Mollisols) — Highest oribatid species richness — thick organic horizons support the full ecomorphological range and provide stable substrate for slow-reproducing K-selected species. Reference community for most productive agricultural contexts.

Clay-rich soils (Vertisols, Cambisols) — High abundance and good form diversity. Clay-associated fine pore network supports euedaphic specialists. Clay soils retain moisture that sustains oribatid activity through dry periods.

Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Lower diversity and abundance due to rapid organic matter mineralisation and poor moisture retention. Epedaphic forms predominate where litter inputs are maintained; euedaphic diversity typically low.

Acid soils (Podzols, pH < 5) — Distinctive acid-tolerant community with specialist genera (Scheloribates, Galumna in surface layers; specialist euedaphic taxa at depth). Community composition differs substantially from neutral agricultural soils — requires separate interpretation and different reference values. [geo]
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Total abundance increases measurably within 2–4 years; euedaphic ecomorphological forms reappear within 3–5 years; species richness increases progressively over 5–10 years under permanent no-till — this is the expected long-term trajectory for genuine soil quality recovery. P39: Abundance increases within 2–3 years of SOC improvement; form diversity increases over 5+ years as SOC accumulates and pore microhabitat complexity recovers; species richness increase is the definitive long-term verification signal, detectable after 5–8 years of consistent SOC building management.
level1_protocol_name: Heat extraction with total mite count — limited community observer protocol
level1_output_metric: Total mite count per 25cm2 × 5cm sample; probable oribatid proportion (%). Highly approximate — treat as qualitative presence/abundance indicator only.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Collembola (Profile 04). Spring and autumn surveys in the same season each year for annual comparability.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn.
level1_equipment: Desk lamp (40W); fine mesh or tea strainer; small container; 70% ethanol; hand lens (10×); field datasheet.
level1_reference: Adapted from simplified heat extraction protocol; Berlese (1905) original heat funnel design.
level1_reference_link: No primary DOI for original Berlese protocol — see ISO 23611-2:2006
level2_protocol_name: Berlese-Tullgren funnel extraction — oribatid ecomorphological form classification (shared extraction with Profile 04)
level2_output_metric: Oribatid abundance (individuals per 100cm3 soil); ecomorphological form proportions (% epedaphic, hemiedaphic, euedaphic); euedaphic form ratio; QBS-ar score contribution from oribatid component combined with Profile 04 Collembola component (Parisi et al. 2005 scoring matrix).
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Profile 04. Combined extraction in a single site visit.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — same as Profile 04.
level2_equipment: As per Profile 04 Level 2 — same Berlese-Tullgren setup. Additional: oribatid mite form reference guide (images of representative epedaphic, hemiedaphic, euedaphic genera from the relevant geographic region).
level2_reference: ISO 23611-2:2006; QBS-ar index: Parisi et al. (2005); ecomorphological form classification: Rusek (1998).
level3_protocol_name: Species-level oribatid mite community assessment — diversity indices and full QBS-ar index
level3_output_metric: Oribatid species richness; Shannon diversity index; Chao1 species richness estimator; full QBS-ar score (all arthropod groups); oribatid-based pH preference class (pollution index); beta diversity (Bray-Curtis between sites and years); proportion of K-selected vs r-selected taxa in the community.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Level 2.
level3_equipment: Compound microscope (100–400×); regional oribatid identification key; Hoyer's mounting medium; dissecting needles; QBS-ar scoring matrix; heavy metal pollution index calculation table (van Straalen & Verhoef 1997).
level3_reference: Weigmann G (2006) Hornmilben (Oribatida). Goecke & Evers, Keltern [geo — Central European key]; van Straalen NM, Verhoef HA (1997); Parisi et al. (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: ISO 23611-2:2006; QBS-ar index: Parisi et al. (2005); ecomorphological form classification: Rusek (1998).

hard_prerequisite: None — earthworms are present (or recoverable) in virtually all agricultural soils with pH above 5.0 and moisture above wilting point
monitoring_stage: Fast–medium (Stage 2 from Year 1). Epigeic and endogeic species respond within 1
response_timescale: Epigeic species (e
soil_type_associations: Clay-rich soils (Vertisols, Cambisols) — Support higher earthworm biomass due to better moisture retention and richer mineral food substrate for endogeic species. Clay-associated aggregates provide microhabitat shelter for egg capsules. Most productive monitoring context for all three guilds (Edwards & Bohlen 1996).

Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Lower earthworm abundance due to reduced moisture retention, lower clay-organic matter binding, and faster organic matter mineralisation. Epigeic species predominate; anecic guild is sparse. Recovery trajectories are slower under sandy soil conditions.

Acid soils (pH < 5.0) — Population collapse below pH 5.0; acid-intolerant species eliminated first. Dendrobaena octaedra (acid-tolerant epigeic) may persist in very acid systems. pH recovery above 5.5 [~] is a prerequisite for community-level recovery (Edwards & Bohlen 1996). [geo]
Waterlogged soils (Gleysols) — Absent or very low abundance where anaerobic conditions persist. Drainage management is a prerequisite for earthworm recovery in waterlogged agricultural soils.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Total abundance increases progressively within 1–3 seasons of tillage reduction; anecic guild (Lumbricus terrestris) re-establishes permanent burrow networks after 3–5 years of consistent no-till management. P04: Epigeic species abundance increases within 1–2 seasons of regular compost application; total abundance increases over 2–3 years as surface organic matter quality improves consistently. P05: Recovery of neutral pH-sensitive endogeic species within 2–3 seasons as soil pH stabilises above 5.5 [~]; population increase is gradual and depends on pH trajectory confirmed by A08 (Soil pH) abiotic precursor. P39: Total abundance and guild diversity increase progressively over 3–5 years as SOC accumulates; anecic guild recovery is the final and most informative biological signal that genuine soil carbon architecture is being rebuilt.
level1_protocol_name: Visual earthworm count — square frame hand-excavation method
level1_output_metric: Earthworm count per 0.0625m² (= per 625cm²) frame; extrapolated to individuals/m². Reported by guild (epigeic, endogeic, anecic count separately). Anecic guild presence (detected/not detected) is the most important qualitative output at this protocol level.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring — when soil temperature at 10cm exceeds 8°C [~] and soil is moist but not waterlogged. This is peak surface-active period for all three guilds in temperate systems. Record soil temperature at time of sampling.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after first autumn rains, when soil moisture returns and temperatures are still above 8°C. Same-season results compared across years only.
level1_equipment: 25cm × 25cm square frame (metal or wood); trowel or small spade; plastic sheet or bucket for soil sorting; field datasheet; camera; soil thermometer.
level1_reference: IBIS field protocol (CIFOR-ICRAF LDSF, 2025); Ecdysis 1000 Farms earthworm sampling protocol; simplified from ISO 23611-1:2006.
level2_protocol_name: Mustard allyl isothiocyanate extraction — ISO 23611-1:2006 adapted
level2_output_metric: Earthworm abundance (individuals/m²) by guild (epigeic, endogeic, anecic); fresh biomass (g/m²) total and by guild. Anecic guild biomass per m² is the primary long-term recovery indicator.
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — soil temperature 8–16°C [~] at 10cm, moist but not saturated. Avoid sampling within 2 weeks of tillage, irrigation, or heavy rainfall events. Soil temperature and moisture must be recorded at sampling.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after first autumn rains. Same restrictions apply.
level2_equipment: 50cm × 50cm metal insertion frame; watering can or sprayer; 1% mustard allyl isothiocyanate solution (20 litres per site); trowel; plastic sheet; field balance (precision ±0.1g); ID reference card (three guilds); field datasheet; camera.
level2_reference: ISO 23611-1:2006 Soil quality — Sampling of soil invertebrates — Part 1: Hand-sorting and formalin extraction of earthworms.
level3_protocol_name: Earthworm DNA metabarcoding — COI barcoding
level3_output_metric: Species list per site; guild composition (%); Shannon diversity index; Chao1 species richness estimate; beta diversity (Bray-Curtis between sites and years). Anecic species richness and relative abundance are the primary long-term diversity recovery indicators.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Level 2. Pair DNA sampling with mustard extraction for cost and logistical efficiency.
level3_equipment: PowerSoil DNA extraction kit; PCR thermocycler; Illumina sequencing access (outsourced or in-house); bioinformatics pipeline and earthworm reference database. Field: same as Level 2 for sample collection.
level3_reference: Elbrecht V et al. (2017) COI metabarcoding primer reference — see References. BOLD Systems earthworm reference library.
primary_reference: ISO 23611-1:2006 Soil quality — Sampling of soil invertebrates — Part 1: Hand-sorting and formalin extraction of earthworms.

monitoring_stage: Fast–medium (Stage 2 from Year 1). Gastropod community composition responds with
response_timescale: Abundance changes in surface-active slug guilds respond with
soil_type_associations: Calcareous soils (Rendzinas, chalk-derived Cambisols) — Highest snail species richness — abundant dissolved calcium supports diverse shell-forming species including large Helicidae (Helix pomatia, Cepaea species) that are entirely absent from acid soils. Priority monitoring context for gastropod biodiversity (Wäreborn 1970; Waldén 1981). [geo — temperate European]

Clay-rich neutral soils (Cambisols, Luvisols, pH 5.5–7.0) — Moderate to good snail diversity — supports intermediate calcium-dependent species. Most productive monitoring context for tracking management-driven community change.

Acid soils (Podzols, pH < 5.5) — Very low snail species richness — calcium limitation restricts communities to a few acid-tolerant species (Oxychilus species, Nesovitrea hammonis). Slug diversity may be relatively unaffected by pH but species composition differs.

Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Low gastropod abundance due to rapid moisture loss — dry sandy soils are physiologically inhospitable. Communities present but sparse; monitoring most productive after rainfall events.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Non-target snail species richness increasing in field margins within 2–4 seasons of molluscicide reduction; decomposer guild recovery in soil fauna within 1–3 seasons; pest slug populations (Deroceras reticulatum, Arion species) may initially increase following molluscicide cessation — damage threshold monitoring recommended in arable systems to determine whether management adjustment is needed. P01: Total gastropod abundance (all guilds combined) increasing measurably within 1–2 seasons of tillage cessation; soil-dwelling guild specifically increases as egg survival improves; snail species richness in associated field margins increases over 3–5 years as surface conditions improve.
level1_protocol_name: Gastropod visual search transect — community observer count and guild classification
level1_output_metric: Total snail count (per 50m transect); total slug count; snail size class distribution; presence of large shell-bearing species (named if identifiable); feeding damage score (0 = none / 1 = trace / 2 = moderate / 3 = heavy) in arable contexts.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring to early summer (April–June in temperate Europe) — when gastropods are most active after winter dormancy and populations reflect winter survival and spring reproduction. Also: September–October for peak surface-active slug populations before autumn crop emergence in arable contexts.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — particularly important for arable pest slug assessment before drilling of autumn crops.
level1_equipment: Headtorch (for dusk survey); photographic gastropod identification guide (regional); field datasheet; camera; ruler (for shell size measurement).
level1_reference: Simplified from standard malacological transect survey methodology; Glen DM et al. (2006) slug damage threshold reference — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Refuge trap survey — standardised tile trap counts for gastropod abundance assessment
level2_output_metric: Mean slug abundance per trap per night; mean snail abundance per trap per night; slug species composition (colour-form classification); damage threshold index (arable contexts); snail species list from combined visual and trap survey.
level2_seasonal_primary: Autumn — September to October in temperate Europe, for the pre-drilling pest risk assessment window. Additionally: spring (April–May) for post-winter community recovery assessment.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Spring — for biodiversity monitoring and seasonal comparison.
level2_equipment: 10 × 30cm × 30cm plywood or slate tiles (or corrugated cardboard); GPS or permanent marker posts for trap positions; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Full malacological survey with soil sampling and metabarcoding
level3_output_metric: Full species list (live + dead shell assemblage); species richness; Shannon diversity; soil-dwelling species richness; metabarcoding-derived slug species list; calcium index (species richness of calcium-dependent species as % of total).
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring — for active animals. Dead shell analysis can be conducted year-round.
level3_equipment: Soil corer (10cm diameter); buckets for flotation; 1mm and 0.5mm sieves; binocular microscope (10–40×); regional malacological identification key; eDNA extraction kit and sequencing access.
level3_reference: Barker GM (2004); Müller J et al. (2020); Wäreborn I (1970) — see References.
primary_reference: Barker GM (2004); Müller J et al. (2020); Wäreborn I (1970) — see References.

response_timescale: Nest density increase in no-till fields detectable within 2–
soil_type_associations: Light, free-draining soils (Arenosols, sandy Cambisols) — Warmest soils with best drainage — highest ant nest density and most diverse communities in temperate systems. Sandy south-facing slopes support the highest diversity in temperate European contexts [geo].

Clay-rich soils (Vertisols) — Lower nest density — clay soils are harder to excavate and crack during dry periods disrupting nest structures. Litter-nesting species predominate over soil-nesting engineers.
Waterlogged soils (Gleysols) — Very low or absent ant communities — waterlogging creates anaerobic nest conditions intolerable to most species. Communities recover where drainage improvement occurs.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Nest density increase detectable within 2–4 years of no-till adoption; functional group diversity increasing over 4–7 years; turf mound presence (where Lasius flavus [geo] is present) after 5–10 years of stable management is a particularly meaningful long-term verification signal. P13: Turf mound condition improving (mound height and extent increasing) within 2–3 years of appropriate stocking management; nest density increasing across the monitoring area within 3–5 years of reduced grazing pressure and improved rest periods.
level1_protocol_name: Ant nest mound count and activity assessment — community observer survey
level1_output_metric: Total nest mound count per monitoring area; turf mound count (permanent grassland only); functional group composition (% of nests assignable to each functional group); active trail presence (Y/N); estimated % area with ant activity.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to midsummer (May–July in temperate Europe) — when ant foraging activity is at its peak and workers are most visible. Avoid survey in cold or wet conditions when ant activity is suppressed.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer (August–September) — for supplementary activity assessment and detection of flying ant emergence events that confirm colony reproductive success.
level1_equipment: GPS; measuring tape (10m); ranging stick; field datasheet; camera; photographic ant functional group reference guide (regional).
level1_reference: Dauber J et al. (2005); Folgarait PJ (1998) — see References; Ecdysis 1000 Farms ant monitoring protocol.
level2_protocol_name: Pitfall trap survey with nest density count — quantitative ant community assessment
level2_output_metric: Ant species richness per trap set; activity index (workers per trap per day); functional group composition (% of catch); morphospecies diversity (Shannon index); nest density per monitoring area (from combined Level 1 count).
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to midsummer — same as Level 1. Avoid rain periods (close traps or remove during rain to prevent drowning and dilution).
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late summer — supplementary trap deployment for seasonal community comparison.
level2_equipment: 5 × 7cm diameter pitfall trap cups; propylene glycol; rain shelter plates; 70% ethanol storage vials; labels; regional ant identification key (e.g. Seifert 2018 for European species [geo]); binocular microscope (10–40×); field datasheet.
level3_protocol_name: Full myrmecological community assessment with nest excavation and molecular identification
level3_output_metric: Full species list; colony density (colonies per m2); mean colony size per species; species richness; Shannon diversity; functional group index (proportion of specialist to generalist species); reproductive success index.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring (nest excavation); July–August for nuptial flight monitoring.
level3_equipment: Trowel and small spade for nest excavation; aspirator; magnifying glass; 95% ethanol for tissue preservation; PCR and sequencing access; camera.
level3_reference: Folgarait PJ (1998); Andersen AN (1995); Hoffmann & Andersen (2003); Dauber et al. (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: Folgarait PJ (1998); Andersen AN (1995); Hoffmann & Andersen (2003); Dauber et al. (2005) — see References.

conditionality_criteria: Dung beetles require vertebrate dung as larval food and brood substrate — they are absent from landscapes with no livestock or wild vertebrates producing sufficient accessible dung. The profile is universally relevant wherever livestock are present within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. In crop-only systems with no livestock component, this profile should be flagged as out-of-scope and excluded from the monitoring programme output.
hard_prerequisite: Livestock (cattle, sheep, horses, pigs, or equivalent) present within or adjacent to the monitored landscape, with dung deposited on accessible ground surfaces. Dung beetle monitoring is not meaningful where dung is collected or covered within hours of deposition. Monitoring note: dung quality matters — dung from livestock treated with macrocyclic lactone antiparasitic drugs (ivermectin, doramectin, moxidectin) within 30 days prior to the monitoring period is toxic to dung beetles and unsuitable as monitoring bait.
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Dung beetle community abundance responds within 1–2
response_timescale: Dung beetle abundance and activity-density in traps increase
soil_type_associations: Deep loam soils (Cambisols, Phaeozems) — Highest tunneller diversity — deep loam soils provide the structural stability and drainage that tunneller species require for nest chamber construction to 20–30cm depth. Tunneller biomass and species richness highest on well-structured loam soils.
Sandy soils (Arenosols) — Moderate to good tunneller communities — easy to excavate but less structurally stable for deep chambers. Dweller species may be proportionally more common.

Compacted clay soils (heavy Vertisols) — Lowest tunneller diversity — clay compaction mechanically prevents deep nest chamber construction. Dweller species (Aphodius) dominate; tunneller species limited to periods of optimal soil moisture when clay softens sufficiently for excavation.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Dung beetle total abundance measurably increasing within 1–2 seasons of reduced ivermectin-family use (dependent on recolonisation from adjacent untreated populations); tunneller guild species appearing within 2–4 seasons; species richness recovery toward reference community within 3–7 years of consistent targeted antiparasitic management. P13: Tunneller guild species appearing in pitfall traps within 2–3 seasons of stocking rate reduction; nest chamber presence detectable in soil samples within 2–4 seasons; tunneller biomass (as proportion of total dung beetle catch) increasing progressively over 3–5 years of consistent rotational management.
level1_protocol_name: Fresh dung pat observation — dung beetle visitor count and burial activity assessment
level1_output_metric: Mean dung beetle count per 10-minute pat observation; proportion of pats showing tunnelling activity (%); 24-hour burial rate (% original volume remaining).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–August in temperate Europe) — peak dung beetle flight and foraging activity. Surveys must be conducted on warm days (air temperature >15°C [~]) when flight activity is occurring. Cold or rainy days produce very low beetle counts regardless of community health.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — secondary flight activity peak in many temperate species.
level1_equipment: GPS; watch or phone timer; field datasheet; camera. No specialist equipment. Disposable gloves recommended for handling dung pats.
level1_reference: Ecdysis 1000 Farms dung beetle rapid assessment protocol; Nichols et al. (2008) service delivery assessment — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Dung-baited pitfall trap survey — quantitative abundance and functional guild assessment
level2_output_metric: Total dung beetle abundance per trap per day; morphospecies richness per transect; functional guild proportions (% tunneller / dweller / roller); dominant species or morphospecies identity.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–August in temperate Europe) — same as Level 1. Traps are not affected by temperature as much as direct observation, but activity-density is highest in warm conditions.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — same as Level 1.
level2_equipment: 15cm plastic cups; mesh bag for dung bait (5cm × 5cm mesh); fresh dung (standardised source); water and washing-up liquid; dissecting microscope (10–40×); regional dung beetle identification key; 70% ethanol vials; forceps; GPS; field datasheet.
level3_protocol_name: Full dung beetle community assessment with nest chamber excavation and burial rate quantification
level3_output_metric: Full species list; nest chamber density (chambers per m2); mean excavation depth; burial rate (g/hour/m2) for exclosure vs open control; species-level abundance and functional guild; isotope-confirmed dietary positions.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — peak activity and nest chamber density.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: wire mesh exclosures; calibrated balance; soil excavation tools; isotope laboratory access; species-level regional key (European: Lobo et al. 2006 [geo]; tropical: regional specialist key required).
level3_reference: Nichols et al. (2008); Lumaret et al. (1993); Verdú et al. (2015) — see References.
primary_reference: Nichols et al. (2008); Lumaret et al. (1993); Verdú et al. (2015) — see References.

monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Pitfall trap activity-density responds within 1–2 se
response_timescale: Total activity-density (individuals per trap per day) increa
soil_type_associations: Sandy, well-drained soils — Highest carabid species richness — thermophilous species particularly diverse; deep burrowing by larvae possible. Many specialist brachypterous species are sand-associated.
Heavy clay soils — Lower species richness but high activity-density of Pterostichus species in wet periods. Clay soils favour moisture-tolerant generalist species; specialist species reduced.
Peaty or organic soils — High staphylinid diversity; specialist hygrophilous carabid species associated with wet organic soils in drainage channels and field edges.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Total pitfall trap activity-density (individuals per trap per day) measurably increasing within 1–2 seasons of insecticide cessation; predatory large-bodied species (Pterostichus, Carabus) appearing within 2–3 seasons; brachypterous specialist species richness increasing over 3–5 years as specialist populations recolonise from adjacent source habitats. P01: Pitfall trap activity-density increasing in spring within 1–2 seasons of no-till adoption; larger-bodied Pterostichus species (requiring intact larval habitat) increasing in activity within 2–4 seasons; full specialist community recovery requires 5+ years of consistent no-till combined with field margin quality improvement.
level1_protocol_name: Refuge tile search — ground beetle and rove beetle daytime shelter count
level1_output_metric: Number of ground beetles per tile (size classes recorded); number of rove beetles per tile; presence of large carabid species (>15mm — most ecologically significant predators); combined beetles per tile per night.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring to early summer (April–July in temperate Europe) — when carabids are most active after winter. Avoid very cold nights (<5°C) when beetles are inactive.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — when adults are active before winter shelter seeking.
level1_equipment: 10 refuge tiles (30cm × 30cm, same as Profile 07); field datasheet; hand lens (10×); camera. No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Pfiffner L, Luka H (2003) — see References; shared tile protocol with Profile 07.
level2_protocol_name: Pitfall trap survey — ground beetle and rove beetle activity-density and morphospecies assessment
level2_output_metric: Total carabid activity-density (individuals per trap per day); total staphylinid activity-density; carabid morphospecies richness per transect; large predatory species proportion (% of catch >15mm); brachypterous:macropterous species ratio [~] where species-level identification is possible.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–July in temperate Europe) — peak adult activity. Secondary: autumn (September–October). Avoid mid-summer dry periods when activity drops and moulting periods that bias body condition.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — particularly important for detecting autumn-breeding species (many Carabus, Pterostichus species breed in autumn in temperate Europe [geo]).
level2_equipment: 10 pitfall trap cups (7–10cm diameter); propylene glycol; small trap roofs; dissecting microscope (10–40×); regional carabid reference guide; 70% ethanol vials; forceps; field datasheet.
level3_protocol_name: Full species-level carabid and staphylinid assessment with wing morph and connectivity analysis
level3_output_metric: Full species list; wing morph ratio; trophic position diversity from isotope analysis; genetic diversity (Fst between sites) as connectivity indicator; species-level activity-density.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring (May–July) and autumn (September–October) deployments, both essential for complete species capture.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: regional species-level Carabidae and Staphylinidae keys; isotope laboratory; molecular genetics laboratory.
level3_reference: Kromp B (1999); Lövei GL, Sunderland KD (1996); Thiele HU (1977) — see References.
primary_reference: Kromp B (1999); Lövei GL, Sunderland KD (1996); Thiele HU (1977) — see References.

monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Spider activity-density in pitfall traps responds wi
response_timescale: Wolf spider (Lycosidae) activity-density in field margin pit
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Spider activity-density (all species combined) measurably increasing within 1–2 seasons of insecticide cessation; wolf spider (Lycosidae) species appearing within 2–3 seasons; functional group diversity (web-builders and hunters both present) recovering within 2–4 seasons; full species richness recovery requires 3–5 years of consistent improved management combined with adjacent habitat quality. P16: Margin pitfall trap activity-density increasing within 1–2 seasons of cutting regime improvement; headland (crop-edge) activity-density increasing within 2–3 seasons as margin source population expands; species richness increasing over 3–5 years as diverse margin vegetation structure develops.
level1_protocol_name: Refuge tile search and web count — community observer spider assessment
level1_output_metric: Spider count per tile (hunters vs web-builders recorded separately); egg sac presence (Y/N per tile); web count per 10m vegetation strip.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring (May–June) — wolf spider females carrying egg sacs are most visible; web-building species are establishing new webs after winter. Web count most reliable in dry, calm weather in the morning after dew has evaporated.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — peak web-building spider density before winter; wolf spider egg sacs of the second generation present.
level1_equipment: 10 refuge tiles (shared with Profiles 07 and 10); field datasheet; camera. No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Marc et al. (1999); Sunderland & Samu (2000) — see References; shared tile protocol with Profiles 07 and 10.
level2_protocol_name: Pitfall trap survey with optional suction sampling — combined ground and canopy spider assessment
level2_output_metric: Spider activity-density from pitfall traps (individuals per trap per day); functional group proportions (%); web-building vs hunting ratio; suction sample spider density (individuals per m2 vegetation) where applicable; family-level composition.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring (May–July) — peak wolf spider activity; good web-builder canopy representation. Autumn (September–October) — peak linyphiid canopy abundance (second generation).
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — same as Profile 10.
level2_equipment: As Profile 10 Level 2 (shared pitfall trap equipment). Additional: D-vac or equivalent suction sampler (optional); dissecting microscope; regional spider identification guide.
level3_protocol_name: Full species-level spider community assessment with canopy and ground-layer quantification
level3_output_metric: Full species list; species richness; Shannon diversity; functional group species richness; web-type diversity (sheet / orb / cobweb / no web); linyphiid density (individuals per m2 vegetation from suction sampling); isotope trophic position diversity.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring (May–July) for wolf spiders and hunting species. Autumn (September–October) for linyphiids (peak abundance).
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: regional Araneae species-level key; compound microscope (400×) for small linyphiid identification; stable isotope laboratory.
level3_reference: Nyffeler & Birkhofer (2017); Marc et al. (1999); Schmidt & Tscharntke (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: Nyffeler & Birkhofer (2017); Marc et al. (1999); Schmidt & Tscharntke (2005) — see References.

response_timescale: Pan trap parasitoid abundance increases measurably within 1–
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P23: Pan trap parasitoid abundance measurably higher in the first season of umbellifer flowering establishment compared to baseline; parasitism rate in adjacent crop aphid colonies increases within 2–3 seasons of consistent umbellifer habitat provision; parasitoid species richness in pan traps increases over 3–5 years as diverse floral resources sustain diverse community establishment. P11: Pan trap abundance increasing within 1–2 seasons of insecticide reduction where adequate floral resources are present; parasitism rate in crop host colonies increasing within 2–4 seasons; full community recovery (species richness approaching reference) requires 3–5 years of consistent insecticide-free management combined with adequate floral resource provision.
level1_protocol_name: Umbellifer floral resource assessment and flower visitor count — community observer habitat and utilisation survey
level1_output_metric: Umbellifer cover class in monitoring section; composite flower cover class; small wasp-like visitor count per 2-minute observation (mean across observed plants); combined floral resource quality score (presence of both umbellifer and composite species = high; one type = moderate; neither = absent).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (June–August in temperate Europe) — when umbellifer plants are in peak flowering and parasitoid adult activity is highest. Agricultural management context: assess after the main umbellifer flowering period has begun; do not assess within 2 weeks of margin mowing.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Not applicable — umbellifer flowering is the primary and definitive monitoring window.
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; camera; hand lens (10× — optional for close examination of flower visitors). No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Wratten SD et al. (2003); Landis DA et al. (2000) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Yellow pan trap (Moericke trap) survey — parasitoid Hymenoptera abundance and morphospecies assessment
level2_output_metric: Parasitoid Hymenoptera abundance per trap per day; morphospecies richness per deployment; family-level proportions (%); total parasitoid Hymenoptera catch as natural enemy community index.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (June–August in temperate Europe) — coinciding with peak parasitoid activity and umbellifer flowering. Trap catches are strongly weather-dependent; note air temperature and sunshine hours during deployment.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Not formally required — one primary window per year is the minimum. Second deployment in May (pre-umbellifer season) useful for catching overwintered individuals.
level2_equipment: 5 × yellow plastic bowls (20cm diameter, UV-stable yellow); wooden stakes (50–70cm); unscented washing-up liquid; 70% ethanol vials; dissecting microscope (10–40×); forceps; field datasheet.
level3_protocol_name: Species-level parasitoid survey with host colony parasitism rate assessment
level3_output_metric: Parasitoid species list; species richness; Shannon diversity; parasitism rate (%) per host species per monitoring site; host-parasitoid network complexity index (degree, connectance, nestedness).
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — same as Level 2. Aphid colony collection timing coincides with peak cereal aphid season (May–June in temperate European context [geo]).
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: rearing containers (individual vials or small tubes); regional Braconidae and Ichneumonidae species keys; Yu et al. (2016) online host-parasitoid database for cross-referencing.
level3_reference: Landis et al. (2000); Wratten et al. (2003); Tylianakis et al. (2006); Stireman et al. (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: Landis et al. (2000); Wratten et al. (2003); Tylianakis et al. (2006); Stireman et al. (2005) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — monitoring is applicable both where wild bees are present (community assessment) and where they are absent (confirming habitat failure as a driver of absence).
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Flower visit rate and bumblebee abundance respond wi
response_timescale: Bumblebee flower visit rate and total bee visit rate at wild
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P23: Bumblebee and total bee visit rate measurably higher in the first flowering season of wildflower strip establishment compared to baseline; solitary bee species richness increasing over 2–4 seasons as diverse foraging resources sustain diverse forager communities; cleptoparasitic bee species appearing within 3–5 years as host bee community diversity reaches sufficient density. P11: Bumblebee colony indicators (worker abundance at wildflower strips) increasing within 1–2 seasons of neonicotinoid reduction; solitary bee nesting activity increasing over 2–4 seasons; total species richness recovery requires 3–7 years of consistent reduced-neonicotinoid management combined with adequate floral and nesting resource provision.
level1_protocol_name: Wild bee flower visitor transect with ground-nesting habitat assessment — community observer survey
level1_output_metric: Total wild bee count per transect per visit (bumblebees and solitary bees combined, excluding honeybees); bumblebee count separately; solitary bee count separately; plant species visited (list); bare soil patch presence (Y/N) and area (m2); cavity structure presence (Y/N). Trend metric: wild bee count per transect visit across same-season surveys.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–July in temperate Europe) — peak bumblebee worker foraging and peak solitary bee activity. For crop pollination monitoring: during crop bloom (apple bloom = April–May; OSR bloom = April–May; clover bloom = June–August — timing varies by region [geo]).
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer (August–September) — late-season bumblebee queens and late solitary bee species foraging.
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; camera; 50m tape measure (for transect marking). No specialist equipment. Photographic bee functional group reference guide (regional) strongly recommended.
level1_reference: UK Pollinator Monitoring Scheme (PoMS) transect methodology; Potts SG et al. (2010); Pywell RF et al. (2005) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Standardised flower visitor transect with functional group identification and pan trap survey
level2_output_metric: Total wild bee count per transect per visit; bumblebee species or colour form composition; solitary bee genera present; cleptoparasitic bee presence (Y/N — positive community maturity indicator); pan trap bee abundance per species where identifiable; nest entrance hole density (per m2 bare soil); cavity structure count.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — same as Level 1. Pan trap deployment during peak forager activity.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late summer for autumn-active species.
level2_equipment: Regional bee identification guide (photographic); camera with macro capability; 15 pan traps (yellow, blue, white bowls); dissecting microscope (10–40×); GPS; field datasheet; 70% ethanol vials.
level3_protocol_name: Full species-level wild bee survey with pollination service quantification
level3_output_metric: Full species list; species richness; Shannon diversity; pollen specialisation index (proportion of oligolectic specialist species); landscape resource availability index (floral resource area within species-specific foraging ranges); fruit/seed set % in bee-accessible vs bee-excluded treatments (pollination service value quantification).
level3_seasonal_primary: Full season coverage (April–September in temperate Europe [geo]) — one 30-minute netting session per month minimum.
level3_equipment: Aerial insect net; dissecting microscope for pollen; compound microscope (400×) for pollen identification; pollen reference slides; GPS; fruit set counting equipment; regional bee identification keys.
level3_reference: Garratt et al. (2014); Ricketts et al. (2008); Ollerton et al. (2011) — see References.
primary_reference: Garratt et al. (2014); Ricketts et al. (2008); Ollerton et al. (2011) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — however, hoverfly monitoring is most ecologically meaningful where flowering plants are present within or adjacent to the monitoring area.
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Adult abundance at flowers responds within 1 season
response_timescale: Adult hoverfly abundance at flowering plants increases withi
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P23: Hoverfly adult abundance at flowers detectable and measurably higher than baseline in the first flowering season of wildflower strip establishment; Episyrphus balteatus and other aphidophagous species present in the first season; species richness increasing over 2–4 years as diverse larval habitats also develop adjacent to the strip. P14: Hoverfly adult abundance measurably higher in delayed-cut margins within the first season of practice change; comparison of cut vs uncut sections of the same margin provides the clearest in-season verification signal.
level1_protocol_name: Hoverfly flower visitor count and photographic species record — community observer survey
level1_output_metric: Total hoverfly count per 2-minute observation per plant (mean across monitored plants); morphotype count (number of distinct hoverfly types identified); number of photographic records submitted to recording scheme; identified species list (post-survey from photographs).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–August in temperate Europe) — peak adult abundance. Early spring (March–April) also relevant for overwintering Eristalis species foraging on early-season flowers. Same survey window as Profiles 12 and 13.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — second generation Episyrphus balteatus peak in some years.
level1_equipment: Camera with macro capability (smartphone adequate); field datasheet. No additional equipment beyond the shared flower visitor count protocol.
level1_reference: HoverFly Recording Scheme (UK) photographic identification resources [geo]; Speight MCD (2017) species accounts; shared flower visitor count protocol with Profiles 12 and 13.
level2_protocol_name: Pan trap survey and morphospecies identification — quantitative hoverfly community assessment
level2_output_metric: Syrphidae abundance per trap per day; morphospecies richness; aphidophagous guild proportion (% of Episyrphus, Syrphus, Scaeva-type morphotypes); saprotrophic guild proportion (% of Eristalis, Helophilus-type morphotypes).
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (June–August) — same as Profiles 12 and 13.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — second generation peak.
level2_equipment: Shared pan trap equipment with Profiles 12 and 13. Additionally: dissecting microscope (10–40×); hoverfly morphospecies reference guide (Speight 2017 or regional equivalent); forceps; 70% ethanol vials.
level3_protocol_name: Species-level hoverfly community survey with larval habitat characterisation
level3_output_metric: Full species list; species richness; Shannon diversity; larval guild proportions; hoverfly egg density per aphid colony (eggs per 100 aphids [~]); wet habitat extent (m2) per larval guild type.
level3_seasonal_primary: Three visits essential: early spring (overwintered Eristalis, early Episyrphus), summer (peak diversity), late summer (second generation).
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: insect net; pinning and setting equipment or 70% ethanol collection; Speight (2017) species accounts; hand lens (10×) for larval egg identification.
level3_reference: Speight MCD (2017); Haenke et al. (2009); Jauker & Wolters (2008); Ssymank et al. (2008) — see References.
primary_reference: Speight MCD (2017); Haenke et al. (2009); Jauker & Wolters (2008); Ssymank et al. (2008) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — however, butterfly transect monitoring is most ecologically meaningful where some flowering vegetation is present. In fully enclosed, bare-soil, or permanently flooded landscapes, expected outcome is confirmed absence.
response_timescale: Generalist butterfly species (common whites, meadow browns,
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Generalist butterfly abundance (meadow brown, common whites, small tortoiseshell) measurably higher in the first season of delayed cutting; specialist species beginning to appear within 3–5 seasons as suitable larval host plant habitat develops under consistent management; total transect abundance index increasing progressively over 5 years. P23: Generalist butterfly forager abundance increasing from baseline within 1–2 seasons of flower establishment; host-plant specialist species appearing within 3–7 seasons as host plant populations establish and larval populations build; species richness increase correlates with host plant diversity in the seed mix — this is a practice-specific verification with high interpretability. P16: Generalist species abundance increasing within 1–2 seasons of buffer strip establishment where some floral resource is present; specialist species appearing within 4–7 seasons as strip habitat matures; species richness approaching reference community (semi-natural grassland equivalents) within 7–10 years of consistent management.
level1_protocol_name: Standardised butterfly transect — community observer count
level1_output_metric: Total butterfly count per 50m section per visit (primary metric for abundance index); species list per visit; presence/absence of farmland specialist indicator species [geo — regional list required]; farmland butterfly abundance index (sum of counts across all visits and sections).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–July in temperate Europe) — peak flight season for most farmland species. Survey on warm, calm, sunny days only.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer (August–September) — second brood species and migrants. Equally important in many farming systems.
level1_equipment: Photographic butterfly identification guide (regional — Field Studies Council guides for UK [geo]; regional equivalents for other contexts); GPS; field datasheet; camera.
level1_reference: UK Butterfly Monitoring Scheme (UKBMS) transect methodology — Pollard & Yates (1993); European Butterfly Monitoring Scheme (eBMS) [geo — European context].
level2_protocol_name: Full Lepidoptera community assessment — butterfly transect with day and night moth survey
level2_output_metric: Farmland butterfly abundance index (annual sum across visits × sections); butterfly species richness; specialist:generalist species ratio; day-flying moth species list; night moth species richness and abundance index from light trap; grass-feeding moth proportion (key farmland bird food chain indicator).
level2_seasonal_primary: April–September butterfly transect; May, July, September light trap nights. [geo temperate European context]
level2_seasonal_secondary: September–October — late-season species and moth assessment.
level2_equipment: Regional butterfly field guide (species-level); regional moth identification guide; actinic light trap (15W tube or 125W MV bulb) and associated timer and collection box; killing jar or refrigeration for moth identification; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Full Lepidoptera community assessment with systematic moth monitoring and larval ecology survey
level3_output_metric: Full butterfly and moth species list; population size estimates for key specialist species; larval density (caterpillars per 10m host plant); landscape connectivity index per species; farmland butterfly index.
level3_seasonal_primary: April–September full season survey — all visits and both light trap periods.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: wing-marking pens; landscape GIS software; mark-recapture calculation tools; field guides for larvae and eggs (if available regionally).
level3_reference: Pollard & Yates (1993); Dover & Settele (2009); Van Swaay & Warren (1999) — see References.
primary_reference: Pollard & Yates (1993); Dover & Settele (2009); Van Swaay & Warren (1999) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Permanent or semi-permanent vegetation (grassland, tall margin strip, or scrub-grassland interface) present within or adjacent to the monitoring area. Orthoptera cannot persist in bare cultivated soils — monitoring is not applicable in crop-only systems without any permanent vegetation component.
response_timescale: Total grasshopper acoustic activity increases within 1–2 sea
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Acoustic activity detectable at monitoring visits within the first season of delayed cutting where populations are present; total species count by acoustic detection increasing within 1–3 seasons of consistent improved management; specialist species appearing after 3–7 years of sustained management improvement combined with connectivity to source populations. P13: Total acoustic species count increasing within 2–4 seasons of rotational grazing adoption; specialist grasshopper species (Chorthippus parallelus, Stenobothrus lineatus [geo]) appearing within 3–7 years in landscapes with source populations within dispersal range.
level1_protocol_name: Acoustic detection and sweep net survey — community observer Orthoptera assessment
level1_output_metric: Number of distinct acoustic types detected per survey; sweep net count (grasshoppers + crickets per 20 sweeps); sward height diversity score (1 = uniform, 3 = all three height classes present).
level1_seasonal_primary: Peak adult season: July–September in temperate Europe — when adults are most abundant and acoustically active. In tropical and subtropical systems, timing varies by species and rainy season. Record all visits in the same calendar month each year for comparability.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late June — for early-season species (Gryllus campestris is most vocal in May–June in UK [geo]).
level1_equipment: Standard insect net (30cm diameter); white cloth or sheet; field datasheet; camera; optional: smartphone with a recording app to capture audio for identification support.
level1_reference: Gardiner T, Hill J, Chesmore D (2002) — see References; Orthoptera Recording Scheme (UK) detection methodology [geo].
level2_protocol_name: Standardised sweep net transect with acoustic recording — species-level Orthoptera community survey
level2_output_metric: Species richness per transect visit; abundance per species per 50 sweeps; male acoustic activity density (singing males per minute per recording point); sward height distribution from 20-point measurement.
level2_seasonal_primary: July–September in temperate Europe — peak adult abundance and acoustic activity. Early morning (09:00–12:00) and early afternoon (14:00–17:00) for peak stridulation activity.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late June — for early cricket species.
level2_equipment: Insect net (30cm diameter); white cloth; regional Orthoptera identification guide (voucher photographs); field recorder or smartphone with external microphone; acoustic analysis software access; field datasheet.
level3_protocol_name: Distance sampling population estimation with acoustic point counts and priority species mark-recapture
level3_output_metric: Population density (singing males per hectare) per species; density trend over years; landscape connectivity index (from species occurrence and dispersal modelling); population viability assessment for specialist species.
level3_seasonal_primary: July–September — peak adult abundance. Point count protocol requires consistent warm sunny conditions for all sessions.
level3_equipment: Calibrated recording equipment; DISTANCE software (free, University of St Andrews); non-toxic paint for mark-recapture; fine brushes; regional specialist Orthoptera identification key.
level3_reference: Gardiner et al. (2002); Hochkirch et al. (2016) — see References.
primary_reference: Gardiner et al. (2002); Hochkirch et al. (2016) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Permanent or seasonal water body present within the monitored landscape that holds water at the time of survey. Not applicable where no surface water feature exists.
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). BMWP scores and total taxon richness respond within
response_timescale: Total taxon richness and ASPT (Average Score Per Taxon) impr
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P16: BMWP score increasing within 2–3 seasons of buffer strip establishment and measurably improving A16 (turbidity) and A17 (nitrate); EPT family richness increasing within 3–5 seasons of consistent water quality improvement; ASPT improvement from <4 toward >6 over 3–7 years of sustained buffer strip and catchment management. P13: Gammaridae (freshwater shrimps) present in kick samples within 1–2 seasons of livestock exclusion where upstream source populations exist; BMWP score increasing within 2–3 seasons; EPT families appearing within 3–5 seasons as water quality stabilises and gravel habitat sediment clears.
level1_protocol_name: Simplified kick sampling — Riverfly Partnership community observer protocol
level1_output_metric: Trigger score per station per visit (following Riverfly Partnership protocol [geo]); presence/absence and abundance class of eight key groups; EPT group count (stonefly + mayfly + caddisfly combined score).
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring (March–May) — when EPT populations are highest and nymphal/larval stages are most visible. Autumn (September–October) — second survey for seasonal comparison.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — for seasonal comparison and detection of late-year recruits.
level1_equipment: 500μm mesh pond net; white sampling tray; magnifying hand lens (10×); field datasheet; Riverfly Partnership identification card (laminated); camera.
level1_reference: Riverfly Partnership Riverfly Monitoring Initiative protocol (Riverfly Partnership, 2012) [geo — UK]; modified for farm-level LAHMP monitoring.
level2_protocol_name: Three-minute kick sample with BMWP scoring — trained technician protocol
level2_output_metric: BMWP score per station per visit; ASPT; number of scoring families; EPT family richness; functional feeding group composition (% shredder / scraper / collector / predator).
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring (March–May) — peak EPT populations. Autumn (September–November) — for seasonal comparison and detection of different cohorts.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn.
level2_equipment: 500μm mesh pond net; white sampling trays (3); forceps; hand lens (10×); magnifying glass (optional); regional freshwater invertebrate family identification key; BMWP scoring table; field datasheet; 70% ethanol vials for voucher specimens if needed; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Quantitative macroinvertebrate sampling with multi-metric biological index and eDNA
level3_output_metric: Full species list; quantitative density (individuals/m2) per taxon; multi-metric biological index score; WFD ecological status class; eDNA-detected species list; O/E ratio (observed/expected taxa) from reference condition modelling.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring (March–May) and autumn (September–October) — both seasons required for full community characterisation.
level3_equipment: Surber sampler; 70% ethanol for preservation; filtration pump and 0.45μm membrane filters; laboratory compound microscope (100–400×); comprehensive freshwater invertebrate keys; DNA extraction laboratory access.
level3_reference: Friberg N et al. (2009); Bonada et al. (2006) — see References.
primary_reference: Friberg N et al. (2009); Bonada et al. (2006) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Permanent or semi-permanent water body with confirmed or likely fish presence and adequate habitat for electric fishing or seine netting access. Very small or intermittent water bodies (<1m wide, <10cm deep in summer) may not support fish populations — in these cases the profile generates a 'not applicable — water body too small/ephemeral for fish monitoring' output.
response_timescale: Pollution-tolerant species proportion declining and sensitiv
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P13: Electrofishing or eDNA detection of juvenile (0+ age class) salmonids within 3–5 seasons of livestock exclusion and riparian recovery where adult source population is available; EFI+ or equivalent score improving progressively over 5–10 years; pollution-tolerant:sensitive species ratio improving from Year 3 onward as water quality improves cumulatively. P16: Guild ratio improving (proportion of pollution-sensitive species increasing) within 3–7 seasons of buffer strip establishment; EFI+ or equivalent score improving measurably over 5–10 seasons of consistent buffer management; salmonid juvenile cohorts appearing within 5 years where breeding adults are present and spawning gravel has recovered.
level1_protocol_name: Visual fish observation and eDNA presence/absence — community observer assessment
level1_output_metric: Fish presence (Y/N); size class distribution of observed fish; species identified (visual or eDNA); guild assessment (sensitive species present Y/N; tolerant species present Y/N); water clarity (clear / moderate / turbid — would fish be visible if present?).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (April–July in temperate Europe) — fish most active and visible in shallow water; juvenile fish of the year visible from June onward. eDNA detection reliable year-round but highest signal in summer when fish activity and cell shedding are highest.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–October) — adult salmonids congregating for spawning are most visible from the bank. Juvenile 0+ fish visible in shallow marginal water.
level1_equipment: Polarising sunglasses (essential); field datasheet; camera. For eDNA: sterile 1L sampling bottles; 47mm filter holder and 0.45μm membranes; hand pump; 100% ethanol; labelled sample tubes. Do not touch water with bare hands when collecting eDNA samples — wear disposable gloves.
level1_reference: eDNA fish detection methodology following Rees et al. (2014); bankside observation protocol adapted from LDSF and Ecdysis field assessment protocols.
level2_protocol_name: Standardised single-pass electrofishing with EFI+ scoring or national equivalent biotic index
level2_output_metric: EFI+ score or national equivalent; species list; total fish density (individuals per 100m); biomass (g per 100m); guild ratio (sensitive:tolerant); 0+ juvenile proportion (%) as spawning success indicator; size class distribution per species.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early autumn (May–September in temperate Europe) — when fish are active and visible; avoid spawning periods (autumn–winter for salmonids). Check national protocol for recommended seasonal window.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — particularly for juvenile 0+ salmonid detection (confirms successful spawning this year).
level2_equipment: Electrofishing unit (backpack DC pulse generator); licensed operator; stop-net(s); fish handling equipment (nets, fish container with oxygenated water, anaesthetic if required by national protocol); measuring board; balance; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Multi-pass electrofishing population estimates with eDNA metabarcoding and genetic connectivity analysis
level3_output_metric: Population density estimate (N/100m) with 95% CI per species; capture probability (p); age class proportions; genetic diversity (He, Ho) per site; Fst between sites as connectivity index; eDNA quantification (copies/L) per species; complete species inventory including legally protected species.
