level3_seasonal_primary: As Level 2. Autumn for 0+ juvenile cohort assessment and spawning activity observation.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: stop nets and block nets for 100m section isolation; scale reading equipment or otolith extraction tools; qPCR laboratory access; genetic laboratory access.
level3_reference: Pont et al. (2006); Friberg et al. (2009); Rees et al. (2014) — see References.
primary_reference: Pont et al. (2006); Friberg et al. (2009); Rees et al. (2014) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None for acoustic monitoring. Bat physical handling and roost surveys (Level 3) require a licence in most jurisdictions — confirm before deployment.
response_timescale: Common pipistrelle and noctule activity-density increases me
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Bat activity-density (passes per minute at monitoring transect points) measurably increasing within 2–3 seasons of insecticide reduction; common pipistrelle activity increasing fastest; additional species (noctule, Daubenton's) appearing within 3–5 seasons as foraging habitat quality improves; specialist species (long-eared, horseshoe) appearing within 5–10 years where roost connectivity is adequate. P19: Bat activity increasing along restored hedgerow transect sections within 3–5 years of hedgerow establishment reaching >1.5m height; pipistrelle commuting calls detectable within 2–3 years; additional species using the hedgerow as foraging corridor within 5–7 years as hedgerow matures.
level1_protocol_name: Bat detector walked transect — community observer activity count
level1_output_metric: Total bat passes per 500m transect; passes per 50m section (habitat breakdown); proportion of higher-frequency vs lower-frequency calls; feeding buzz detections over water (Daubenton's indicator); maximum pass rate section (identifies primary foraging habitat within the transect).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–August in temperate Europe) — when bats are most active and insect prey is most abundant. Avoid cold nights (<10°C [~]) when bat activity drops significantly. Survey beginning 20 minutes after local sunset (check local sunset time for each survey).
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer (August–September) — juvenile bats first emerging; maternity roost exit counts possible.
level1_equipment: Heterodyne bat detector (set to 45kHz starting frequency — basic models suitable; examples: Batbox Duet, Magenta Bat 4, or equivalent); field datasheet; watch; torch (red light preferred to preserve night vision); GPS for route marking in Year 1.
level1_reference: National Bat Monitoring Programme walked transect protocol (Battersby 2010) [geo — UK]; Wickramasinghe et al. (2003) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Full-spectrum static detector survey with species identification — automated overnight recording and acoustic analysis
level2_output_metric: Bat passes per hour per monitoring point per night; species list with activity per species; species richness; aerial hawker:edge species:gleaning specialist ratio; seasonal activity pattern (activity per night across deployment period).
level2_seasonal_primary: May–August — same as Level 1. Minimum 3 consecutive deployment nights during primary monitoring window.
level2_seasonal_secondary: August–September — juvenile dispersal; maternity colony exit counts.
level2_equipment: 3–5 autonomous full-spectrum bat recorders; SD cards (minimum 32GB per recorder per deployment); acoustic analysis software licence (Kaleidoscope Pro or equivalent); desktop or laptop for analysis; GPS for detector positioning.
level3_protocol_name: Licensed bat roost count and population assessment with radiotracking
level3_output_metric: Colony emergence count per roost per year; foraging area (ha) per tagged individual; population structure (% juvenile, sex ratio, mass); home range and core foraging area overlap with farm management zones.
level3_seasonal_primary: June–July for maternity colony emergence counts; August for juvenile counts; October for harp trap at swarming sites.
level3_equipment: Bat handling gloves; bat licence (all handling and roost survey activities require legal permit); harp trap; radio transmitters and receiver; GPS; bat ringing equipment (if applicable).
level3_reference: Battersby (2010); Fuentes-Montemayor et al. (2013); Boyles et al. (2011); Wickramasinghe et al. (2003) — see References.
primary_reference: Battersby (2010); Fuentes-Montemayor et al. (2013); Boyles et al. (2011); Wickramasinghe et al. (2003) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — however, the meaningful ecological signal from this profile comes from comparing specialist vs generalist species counts. Where no birds are detectable on surveys (extremely arid, intensively cultivated landscapes with no non-crop habitat features), the monitoring produces a confirmed-absence output which is itself an ecologically meaningful result.
response_timescale: Generalist and semi-specialist farmland species (linnet, yel
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Grey partridge brood size increasing from <3 to >4 chicks per pair [~] within 2–3 breeding seasons of conservation headland establishment; territory count of insectivorous specialists increasing within 3–5 seasons; chick food invertebrate community quality improvement (cross-reference Profiles 10, 11, 14) within 1–2 seasons as the enabling abiotic change. P14: Territory count or nest success measurably higher in delayed-cut sections within the first breeding season of practice change; ground-nesting species (skylark, yellowhammer, lapwing) most responsive; further species accumulation over 3–5 seasons as populations build.
level1_protocol_name: Point count survey — community observer breeding season bird count
level1_output_metric: Total bird species count per point count; specialist farmland bird species count (using regional specialist list); peak count per specialist species (maximum individuals simultaneously recorded at any point count position); breeding behaviour confirmed (Y/N per species).
level1_seasonal_primary: Breeding season: late April to late June in temperate Europe (breeding season peak when territorial males are most vocal) [geo]. Survey must be conducted in the first 3 hours after dawn — dawn chorus activity is dramatically higher than mid-day activity. Do not survey in rain, strong wind, or fog.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Early August — late-summer count including family parties; used for grey partridge brood index (number of chicks per adult pair).
level1_equipment: Regional bird field guide or photographic guide; bird song identification app (BirdNET, Merlin Bird ID, or equivalent); binoculars (7×42 or 8×42 recommended); field datasheet; GPS for point position marking.
level1_reference: Point count methodology from Bibby et al. (2000) simplified for farm monitoring; Farmland Bird Index framework from Pan-European Common Bird Monitoring Scheme.
level2_protocol_name: BBS-style line transect breeding bird survey with territory mapping for key species
level2_output_metric: Species list with territory count (from cluster analysis); specialist farmland bird territory count per 10ha; specialist:generalist species ratio; grey partridge brood index (chicks per pair in August count); BBS-compatible data for national scheme submission.
level2_seasonal_primary: April and June visits (temperate Europe [geo]) — aligned with UK BBS standard timing. Visits must be in the first 2 hours after dawn.
level2_seasonal_secondary: August — late-summer count for grey partridge brood index.
level2_equipment: Regional species-level field guide; binoculars; bird song identification app; 1:5,000 or 1:10,000 field maps for territory mapping; field datasheet following BBS recording code standard; GPS.
level3_protocol_name: Full territory mapping with nest outcome monitoring and chick food invertebrate integration
level3_output_metric: Territory count per species per 10ha; nest success rate (%); brood size at fledging; chick food invertebrate biomass (mg/m2) at nesting territories; population growth rate (lambda); correlation between chick food invertebrate biomass and brood size.
level3_seasonal_primary: March–July for territory mapping and nest monitoring; May–July for chick food sampling.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: GPS-linked recording device for territory mapping; nest recording cards (NRS standard); D-vac or sweep net for invertebrate sampling; invertebrate identification key for chick food groups.
level3_reference: Potts GR (1986); Donald et al. (2001); Wilson et al. (2009); Aebischer & Ewald (2010) — see References.
primary_reference: Potts GR (1986); Donald et al. (2001); Wilson et al. (2009); Aebischer & Ewald (2010) — see References.

conditionality_criteria: Applicable wherever permanent or seasonal water bodies exist within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. Where no suitable breeding water body is available within approximately 2km of the monitoring area, this profile generates a 'no suitable breeding habitat present' output, which is itself ecologically informative.
hard_prerequisite: A permanent or seasonal pond, ditch, wetland, or other standing water feature suitable for amphibian breeding — minimum approximately 4m2 surface area, retaining water for at least the period from February to June in temperate systems [geo]. Road mortality counts and terrestrial transects provide supplementary data where breeding ponds are not accessible for direct assessment.
primary_monitoring_role: Water body and landscape quality indicator
response_timescale: First colonisation of new or improved ponds by common frog —
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P13: Common frog egg mass count increasing within 2–3 seasons of livestock exclusion where adult source population is present within the pond; spawn string count (common toad) stable or increasing within 3–5 seasons; metamorph count detectable in late summer within 2 seasons of improved breeding conditions. P17: First common frog spawn detected within 1–2 springs of pond establishment where source populations within 2km; spawn count increasing progressively over 3–7 seasons as local population builds; common toad populations slower to colonise — confirm establishment within 5–10 seasons.
level1_protocol_name: Breeding pond amphibian spawn count — community observer survey
level1_output_metric: Common frog egg mass count per pond; common toad spawn string length estimate (m); tadpole aggregation score (absent/few/many/very many); metamorph count per bank metre; fish presence (Y/N — critical contextual variable for interpreting tadpole survival).
level1_seasonal_primary: Early spring — late January to March in temperate lowland Europe [geo]; timing varies with altitude and latitude. The spawning window is typically 2–3 weeks long and peaks when overnight air temperatures remain above 5°C for several consecutive nights. Survey within the first week of spawning beginning for the most accurate egg mass count — spawn aggregates rapidly become indistinct as development advances.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late spring/early summer: tadpole and metamorph count (May–June in temperate Europe [geo]).
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; camera; waterproof footwear or waders (for bank edge access only — do not enter the pond). Binoculars helpful for counting in larger ponds.
level1_reference: Amphibian and Reptile Conservation (ARC) Trust great crested newt and common amphibian monitoring methodologies adapted for Level 1 [geo — UK]; Beebee & Griffiths (2005) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Night torch survey with adult count and egg mass survey — quantitative breeding population assessment
level2_output_metric: Adult count per species per torch survey; sex ratio per species; peak count across 3 survey nights; egg mass count; metamorph count per 10m bank; pitfall trap count per trap-night where deployed.
level2_seasonal_primary: January–March for torch survey and egg mass count; June–July for metamorph count.
level2_seasonal_secondary: April–May for late-season tadpole density assessment.
level2_equipment: Powerful waterproof torch (minimum 200 lumens); red light headtorch (preserves night vision for observer); waders or waterproof boots; pitfall trap buckets (10 × 10cm diameter) with drift fence; field datasheet. Biosecurity: Virkon S solution; spray bottle.
level3_protocol_name: Mark-recapture population estimation with eDNA quantification and disease surveillance
level3_output_metric: Population size estimate (N) with 95% CI; eDNA concentration (copies/L); disease prevalence (% individuals positive for Bd/Bsal/ranavirus); eDNA:adult count calibration coefficient.
level3_seasonal_primary: Peak breeding season — January–March for temperate European species.
level3_equipment: Pitfall trap array or torch survey; VIE injection kit or photographic ID system; eDNA filtration equipment; qPCR laboratory; sterile disease swabs; PCR testing laboratory.
level3_reference: Beebee & Griffiths (2005); Griffiths et al. (2010); Relyea (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: Beebee & Griffiths (2005); Griffiths et al. (2010); Relyea (2005) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — applicable in all managed agricultural landscapes with any vegetated habitat component.
response_timescale: Hedgehog track tube detection rate increases within 2–4 seas
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Shrew live trap index measurably higher in uncut vs cut margin sections within 1–2 seasons of cutting delay; hedgehog track tube detection rate increasing within 2–4 seasons as tussock structural complexity develops; mole activity density stable or increasing as soil invertebrate prey improves under reduced disturbance. P11: Hedgehog track tube detection rate improving within 2–4 seasons of rodenticide cessation in the monitoring area; shrew live trap index improving within 1–3 seasons of insecticide reduction as prey invertebrate communities recover; mole activity density increasing progressively as soil invertebrate biomass improves under reduced pesticide loading.
level1_protocol_name: Hedgehog track tube survey, molehill count, and opportunistic night observation
level1_output_metric: Hedgehog footprint detection rate (% of track tubes showing hedgehog prints per deployment); mole activity density (active molehills per 200m2 transect); opportunistic evening hedgehog count per survey km walked.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring to autumn (April–October in temperate Europe) — when hedgehogs are active and molehills are most visible. Track tube survey most reliable May–August. Avoid periods after heavy rain (footprints wash out of tracking film).
level1_seasonal_secondary: Early autumn (September–October) — before hedgehog hibernation begins; last opportunity for evening observation.
level1_equipment: Track tubes (5 × 30cm sections of 10cm-diameter tube — can be made from cardboard or bought); tracking film or footprint pads; bait (peanut butter or cat food paste); sticky tape to hold pads in position; field datasheet; camera; torch for evening observation.
level1_reference: People's Trust for Endangered Species (PTES) hedgehog survey methodology (Roos et al. 2012) [geo]; Churchfield S (1990) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Longworth live trap survey — shrew activity index and species assessment
level2_output_metric: Shrew captures per 100 trap-nights (activity index); species list where identification possible; minimum number of individuals (from mark-recapture if animals individually marked with ear clip or toe webbing pattern); seasonal activity comparison.
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring (April–June) and autumn (August–October) — when shrew populations are at maximum density. Avoid periods of extreme cold (trapping welfare risk) or extended heat (trap temperature risk).
level2_seasonal_secondary: Summer (July–August) — juvenile shrews dispersing.
level2_equipment: 20 Longworth live traps; mealworms; oats; hay; clear plastic observation bags; field datasheet. Ethical approval or licence where required by national regulations.
level3_protocol_name: Small mammal insectivore population density estimation with mark-recapture and isotope analysis
level3_output_metric: Population density estimate (N/ha) with 95% CI per shrew species; capture probability; isotope trophic level; hedgehog genetic diversity (He, Fst between sites); landscape connectivity index.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring (April–May) for population peak assessment.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: ear tags or clipping scissors; isotope laboratory; genetics laboratory; licensed hedgehog handler for tissue sampling.
level3_reference: Churchfield S (1990); Huijser MP, Bergers PJM (2000); Reeve N (1994) — see References.
primary_reference: Churchfield S (1990); Huijser MP, Bergers PJM (2000); Reeve N (1994) — see References.

conditionality_criteria: Applicable wherever vegetated habitat with adequate basking opportunities (open, sun-exposed patches adjacent to cover) exists within the monitoring area. Absent from fully enclosed, heavily shaded, or continuously waterlogged habitats with no open sunny areas.
hard_prerequisite: None — applicable in all agricultural landscapes with any semi-open vegetated habitat. In landscapes with no open, sun-exposed vegetation, the expected monitoring outcome is confirmed absence, which is ecologically informative.
response_timescale: Colonisation of new artificial refugia by common lizard and
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Lizard and slow worm detections at refugia measurably higher in sections with delayed cutting within 2–3 seasons of practice change; species count increasing progressively over 3–5 seasons as population density builds under consistently improved management.
level1_protocol_name: Artificial refugia reptile count and visual basking survey — community observer assessment
level1_output_metric: Count per species per refugium per visit; maximum count across all visits per season per refugium (population index); visual transect count per species per visit; seasonal activity curve (count by month across the season).
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring to late summer (March–September in temperate Europe). Peak basking activity: April–June before vegetation becomes too dense; August–September for late-season basking and juvenile emergence.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late August–September — juvenile common lizards (neonates) appearing; mating adults in September for viviparous species.
level1_equipment: 5–10 artificial refugia (60cm × 60cm roofing felt or corrugated roofing sheets — grey or black); field datasheet; camera. Refugia placed 4–6 weeks before first survey and left permanently in position through the monitoring season.
level1_reference: Amphibian and Reptile Groups UK (ARG UK) standard refugia methodology (Edgar & Bird 2006) [geo]; Gent & Gibson (1998) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Extended refugia survey with photographic individual identification and population index calculation
level2_output_metric: Maximum count per species per season (population index); number of individually identified animals (minimum number alive); photographic recapture rate as population stability indicator; habitat quality index from vegetation assessment.
level2_seasonal_primary: As Level 1. Minimum 6 surveys across the full active season for photographic recapture analysis.
level2_equipment: 10 artificial refugia; camera with macro capability; computer with photographic matching software (or manual matching using printed photographs); vegetation measurement equipment (as Profile 28).
level3_protocol_name: Licensed capture survey with mark-recapture population estimation and genetic connectivity analysis
level3_output_metric: Population size estimate (N) with 95% CI; survival rate (phi) from Cormack-Jolly-Seber; genetic diversity (He, Ho); Fst between sites as connectivity indicator.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring (April–June) — optimal for capture and marking; good basking conditions.
level3_equipment: Reptile handling licence; VIE injection kit; tissue sampling vials; genetics laboratory access.
level3_reference: Edgar & Bird (2006); Gent & Gibson (1998); Beebee & Griffiths (2005 — cross-reference herpetological methodology) — see References.
primary_reference: Edgar & Bird (2006); Gent & Gibson (1998); Beebee & Griffiths (2005 — cross-reference herpetological methodology) — see References.

conditionality_criteria: Applicable wherever suitable semi-open vegetated habitat with adequate prey populations exists within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. Absent from heavily managed, uniformly mown, or highly disturbed habitats with no structural complexity.
hard_prerequisite: None — however, monitoring value is low in landscapes where no suitable basking or sheltering habitat exists. Expected minimum habitat requirement: field margins or hedgerow edges with tussock and some structural cover, within 1km of prey populations (amphibians for grass snakes; lizards and small mammals for adders).
response_timescale: Grass snake colonisation of improved pond margins following
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P17: First grass snake detection at refugia adjacent to restored pond within 3–5 seasons of pond establishment where amphibian breeding populations have recovered; count at refugia increasing progressively over 5–10 seasons as local population builds; egg-laying aggregation detected within 5–7 seasons confirming on-farm reproductive success.
level1_protocol_name: Artificial refugia reptile check and visual transect — community observer snake detection
level1_output_metric: Species presence/absence per monitoring visit; count per species per refugium; maximum seasonal count per species as population index; grass snake egg-laying site confirmed (Y/N); egg-laying site size (number of eggs).
level1_seasonal_primary: Early spring (March–April) — adders and grass snakes emerge from hibernation and bask extensively in warm weather before vegetation becomes dense; highest detectability. Late spring–summer (May–August) for grass snake egg-laying site monitoring.
level1_seasonal_secondary: August–September — juvenile grass snakes and adders hatching/emerging; females of both species are most visible basking in late summer.
level1_equipment: As Profile 23 Level 1 — identical. No additional equipment needed.
level1_reference: Amphibian and Reptile Groups UK (ARG UK) standard methodology (Edgar & Bird 2006) [geo] — shared with Profile 23.
level2_protocol_name: Extended refugia snake survey with egg-laying site systematic assessment
level2_output_metric: As Profile 23 plus: number of grass snake egg-laying sites confirmed; clutch size range; egg-laying site substrate type; adder early-spring maximum count.
level2_seasonal_primary: March–September. Mandatory early-spring visits (mid-March, early April) for adder specifically; late May–June for egg-laying site survey; August–September for juvenile emergence.
level2_equipment: As Profile 23 Level 2. Additionally: thermometer for egg-laying site temperature measurement (optimal: 25–30°C at 5cm depth [~]).
level3_protocol_name: Licensed snake capture survey with PIT-tagging and population estimation
level3_output_metric: Population size estimate (N) with 95% CI; annual survival probability (phi); home range size (ha); seasonal habitat use map relative to farm management zones; genetic diversity and Fst between sites.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring (May–June) for grass snake capture; early spring (March–April) for adder capture during basking period.
level3_equipment: Reptile handling licence; PIT tag implanter; PIT tag reader; radio transmitter implant equipment (veterinary); receiver antenna; genetics laboratory.
level3_reference: Edgar & Bird (2006); Gent & Gibson (1998); Beebee & Griffiths (2005) — see References.
primary_reference: Edgar & Bird (2006); Gent & Gibson (1998); Beebee & Griffiths (2005) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — applicable in all agricultural landscapes. Otter spraint component requires water body presence within or adjacent to monitoring area.
response_timescale: Camera trap detection rate improvement following prey commun
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Camera trap weasel and stoat detection rate increasing within 2–4 seasons of rodenticide cessation in farm buildings and field areas; mustelid species richness increasing progressively as prey communities recover; otter spraint occupancy improvement within 5–10 seasons where water quality is concurrently improving. P16: First otter spraint detection at restored riparian monitoring points within 3–7 seasons of buffer strip establishment where otter source populations exist within the catchment; spraint site density increasing over 5–10 seasons as territory becomes established; breeding evidence (spraint with fish otolith remains near suitable bank holt sites) within 7–15 seasons of sustained management improvement.
level1_protocol_name: Footprint tunnel survey and otter spraint survey — community observer carnivore detection
level1_output_metric: Weasel detection rate (% tunnels with weasel prints per deployment); stoat detection rate (% tunnels with stoat prints); otter spraint site density (sites per 500m); fresh vs old spraint ratio (as territory activity indicator).
level1_seasonal_primary: Year-round — footprint tunnels and spraint surveys are applicable in all seasons. Spraint detection is most reliable in spring (January–April in temperate Europe) when otters are most actively marking territories. Footprint tunnels most productive spring–autumn when mustelid activity is highest.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn (September–November) — stoat and weasel activity peaks as prey populations are highest post-summer breeding.
level1_equipment: Footprint tunnels (5 × 50cm boxes with footprint paper); sardine bait; field datasheet; camera (for photographing footprints); waterproof boots for spraint survey.
level1_reference: Chanin P (2003) Monitoring Otter Populations; Vincent Wildlife Trust methodology — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Camera trap survey — small carnivore species detection and occupancy assessment
level2_output_metric: Occupancy per species per station (Y/N per 28-day deployment); relative abundance index — detections per 100 camera-trap-nights per species; daily activity pattern per species; novel species first detection dates.
level2_seasonal_primary: Year-round monitoring is possible and recommended — seasonal activity patterns are ecologically informative. Spring (March–June) is most productive for mustelid family groups; autumn for stoat/ermine transition in cold climates.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Not applicable — camera traps run continuously.
level2_equipment: 5–10 camera traps (PIR-triggered, minimum 0.5-second trigger speed; night infrared flash; 30+ day battery life; 32GB SD cards); cable locks; mounting equipment; image management software (Wildlife Insights, Camera Base, or equivalent for detection rate calculation).
level3_protocol_name: Live trap mustelid survey with individual marking and genetic analysis
level3_output_metric: Population density estimate (N/km2) per mustelid species; survival probability (phi); genetic diversity (He, Fst between sites); otter eDNA concentration (copies/L) as territory density proxy.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring (March–May) for mustelid live trapping; year-round for eDNA.
level3_equipment: Box live traps; bait; PIT tags and reader; ear notch punch or blood lancet; genetics laboratory; eDNA sampling and qPCR laboratory.
level3_reference: McDonald & Harris (2002); Chanin (2003); Burton et al. (2015) — see References.
primary_reference: McDonald & Harris (2002); Chanin (2003); Burton et al. (2015) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — applicable in all managed agricultural landscapes.
response_timescale: Barn owl nest box occupation and breeding attempt rate impro
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Barn owl nest box inspection revealing occupation within 2–3 seasons of rough grassland restoration adjacent to nest box site; breeding attempt confirmed within 3–5 seasons; brood size increasing over 5–7 seasons as rough grassland quality and extent improve progressively. P11: Brood size measurably higher at nest boxes in monitored areas within 2–4 seasons of rodenticide cessation in farm buildings and fields; adult survival rate improvement detectable from colour-ring or wing-tag resighting within 3–5 seasons; pellet analysis showing dietary diversity maintained (no shift toward species unaffected by rodenticides).
level1_protocol_name: Diurnal raptor point count, barn owl nest box check, and dusk observation — community observer raptor survey
level1_output_metric: Raptor species detected per point count (list); barn owl nest box occupancy per visit (empty/adult/eggs/chicks/count); barn owl brood size (chick count in June–July visit); dusk departure count per 30-minute period.
level1_seasonal_primary: Diurnal raptor point count: same as Profile 20 (April–June). Barn owl nest box: April (occupancy check) and June–July (brood size check). Dusk count: June–July when chicks are large and adults are making maximum foraging trips.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Winter: check nest boxes for barn owl winter roosting (November–February) — roosting without breeding is a positive population monitoring outcome indicating that a bird is using the farm as part of its home range.
level1_equipment: Binoculars (10× minimum for raptor identification); field datasheet; nest box key (if padlocked) — from nest box installation records; torch for nest box interior check.
level1_reference: Taylor IR (1994) Barn Owls; Village A (1990) The Kestrel; shared point count methodology with Profile 20 — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Barn owl nest box programme with pellet analysis and kestrel territory mapping
level2_output_metric: Clutch size; brood size; chicks fledged per pair per year; prey species list from pellet analysis; minimum number of individuals per prey species; kestrel territory count per 2km transect.
level2_seasonal_primary: Nest box: April, June, and August–September (3 visits per breeding season). Pellets: collected at August visit for post-season analysis.
level2_seasonal_secondary: November–February: winter nest box roosting check.
level2_equipment: Nest box programme as Level 1 plus: sealed sample bags for pellet collection; forceps; sorting tray; dissecting microscope (10–20×) or hand lens; small mammal skull and bone identification key (Yalden 1977 or equivalent [geo]).
level3_protocol_name: Licensed nest recording with chick ringing and rodenticide body burden assessment
level3_seasonal_primary: Breeding season — April–August for most farmland raptors and owls in temperate Europe.
level3_equipment: BTO ringing licence; BTO Schedule 1 nest recording endorsement; standard ringing equipment; blood sampling lancet and collection tubes; rodenticide assay laboratory access.
level3_reference: Taylor IR (1994); Newton I (1979); Village A (1990) — see References.
primary_reference: Taylor IR (1994); Newton I (1979); Village A (1990) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Arable cropping (annual or biennial crop cultivation) present within the monitored landscape. Not applicable in exclusively pastoral or permanent crop landscapes.
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Species richness changes in the crop-edge zone detec
response_timescale: First appearance of previously suppressed forb species in th
soil_type_associations: Sandy, free-draining soils (Arenosols, Podzols) — Historically supported the most species-rich arable weed communities in temperate Europe — light, warm soils with good drainage favour many rare archaeophyte species. Priority monitoring context for recovery of rare species where seed banks may still persist (Albrecht et al. 2016). [geo]

Calcareous soils (Rendzinas, calcareous Cambisols) — Calcareous arable soils support distinctive calcicole arable weed communities — including several of the rarest archaeophyte species. High monitoring value in regions where calcareous arable farming is traditional (Meyer et al. 2013). [geo]

Heavy clay soils (Vertisols) — Lower arable weed diversity — wet, heavy soils favour nitrophilous competitive species; fewer specialist arable annuals. Community is typically dominated by a small number of competitive species regardless of management intensity.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P11: Species richness increase detectable within the first growing season of herbicide reduction in the crop headland; new species appearing from surviving seed bank within 1–2 seasons; archaeophyte species appear within 3–7 years where local seed bank populations survive, or not at all where seed bank is depleted. P21: Cover of dominant nitrophilous weed species (Galium aparine particularly) declines progressively over 2–4 growing seasons of N reduction; species richness increases concurrently as competitive release allows suppressed species to establish from the seed bank.
level1_protocol_name: Arable headland flora walkthrough — functional group presence and species count
level1_output_metric: Non-crop plant species count per 50m headland section (primary metric); presence of flowering forbs (Y/N); approximate non-crop cover class; management evidence record.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–June in temperate Europe) — when annual arable weeds are in flower and most identifiable. For winter crops: survey before crop harvest (May–July depending on region). For spring crops: survey at peak crop growth (July–August typically). Agricultural management context: record whether herbicide has been applied in the current growing season and when.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Early autumn — for autumn-germinating species in winter crop contexts (September–October survey captures the early autumn community before winter management).
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; camera; regional wildflower photographic guide (optional but helpful).
level1_reference: Storkey J, Westbury DB (2007) Managing arable weeds for biodiversity — see References; Ecdysis 1000 Farms arable vegetation protocol.
level2_protocol_name: Arable headland community composition survey — species-level quadrat assessment
level2_output_metric: Total species richness per quadrat; forb species richness; Braun-Blanquet cover per species; presence of archaeophyte or conservation-priority species (list); Ellenberg N mean per quadrat [geo]; Shannon diversity index.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — peak crop growth; most arable species identifiable. For winter cereals: late May to late June in temperate Europe [geo]. For spring crops: July–August.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Early autumn — for autumn-germinating species in winter crop headlands (September–October [geo]).
level2_equipment: 0.5m × 0.5m quadrat frame; regional arable weed flora field guide; Braun-Blanquet cover class reference card; GPS; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Full arable weed community assessment with seed bank sampling and in-crop survey
level3_output_metric: Full species list (headland + in-crop); seed bank density per species (seeds/m2); archaeophyte population size and GPS distribution; community classification; seed bank diversity index.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring (flowering peak). Seed bank sampling: early autumn after seed set.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: soil corer (0–10cm); seed germination trays and growth chamber for seed bank germination assay; GPS with sub-metre accuracy; photographic voucher collection.
level3_reference: Albrecht et al. (2016); Meyer et al. (2013); Marshall et al. (2003) — see References.
primary_reference: Albrecht et al. (2016); Meyer et al. (2013); Marshall et al. (2003) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Grassland, pasture, meadow, or semi-natural sward present. This profile is not applicable in landscapes comprising exclusively arable cropland with no permanent grass component.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — primary task type: community composition survey (quadrat-based). See Flora Variant Appendix for additional applicable task type variants.
monitoring_stage: Medium–slow (Stage 2–3). Fast-responding indicators (dominant grass species comp
response_timescale: Legume cover (Trifolium, Lotus, Vicia) measurable within 1–2
soil_type_associations: Calcareous soils (Rendzinas, Luvisols on chalk/limestone) — Support species-richest grassland communities in temperate Europe. Ellenberg R values 7–9 (base-rich) characteristic — indicator species include Salvia pratensis, Briza media, Poterium sanguisorba, Rhinanthus minor. High monitoring value for detecting management-driven diversity loss or recovery. [geo]

Neutral to mildly acid soils (Cambisols, Luvisols) — Support diverse hay meadow and pasture communities. Ellenberg R values 5–7 characteristic. Traditional indicator species: Leucanthemum vulgare, Centaurea jacea, Daucus carota, Trifolium pratense. Most widespread grassland monitoring context in temperate Europe. [geo]

Acid soils (Gleysols, Podzols) pH 4–5.5 — Support heath-grassland communities dominated by grasses (Deschampsia flexuosa, Nardus stricta, Agrostis canina) with few forbs. Very low nitrogen-indicator values characteristic (Ellenberg N 1–3). Require separate botanical expertise for appropriate community assessment. [geo]

Waterlogged / seasonally wet soils (Gleysols) — Support wet meadow and marshy grassland communities (Glyceria, Juncus, Molinia-dominated). Ellenberg F values 7–10 characteristic. High conservation value but require specialist botanical knowledge for community classification.
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P13: Forb presence and cover increase progressively over 2–5 years under managed grazing; competitive dominant grass cover density decreases; indicator species for moderate disturbance (Plantago lanceolata, Trifolium repens) appear within 1–2 seasons. P14: Flowering forb frequency and number of seed-setting forb species increase within 2–3 seasons of delayed cutting; forb cover increases progressively over 5–10 years in degraded swards where seed bank is intact. P21: Dominant grass cover density declines progressively over 3–7 years as available nitrogen falls below competitive dominance threshold; forb species richness per quadrat increases; legume cover (Trifolium, Lotus) measurable within 1–3 seasons as competitive advantage of grasses reduces (Mountford et al. 1993). P23: Seeded species presence detectable within 1–2 seasons of seeding in suitable soil conditions; persistence over 3+ seasons confirms soil conditions have shifted below the nitrogen threshold for competitive exclusion.
level1_protocol_name: Grassland structured walkthrough — functional group presence and cover assessment
level1_output_metric: Forb presence (number of forb species detected along transect — primary diversity metric); legume presence (yes/no, genera present); dominant grass species; bare soil presence; flowering species count on survey date. Comparison of forb species list across years is the primary trend indicator.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — when the full forb and grass community is in active growth and flowering species are most identifiable. Agricultural management context: 3–5 weeks before the anticipated first hay cut, or 2–3 weeks after livestock removal from the field. Avoid surveying immediately after cutting or intensive grazing.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer / early autumn — after first flush of summer growth following cutting or grazing. Allows detection of autumn-flowering species and assessment of secondary flush diversity.
level1_equipment: 50m tape measure; permanent marker posts (Year 1 setup); field wildflower photographic guide (national or regional); camera; field datasheet.
level1_reference: Ecdysis 1000 Farms grassland structured walkthrough protocol; IUCN LAHMP field protocol — Level 1 grassland.
level2_protocol_name: Braun-Blanquet quadrat community composition survey
level2_output_metric: Forb species richness per quadrat (primary indicator); total vascular plant species richness; Shannon diversity index (H'); Braun-Blanquet cover class per species and functional group; Ellenberg indicator values — mean L, F, R, N, S per quadrat — for soil-habitat proxy analysis [geo temperate European only].
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring — in temperate European systems, late May to early July when the full grassland community is in growth and the majority of diagnostic forb species are above ground and identifiable. Agricultural management context: ideally 4 weeks before first hay cut, or after 3+ weeks of grazing exclusion for resting period.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late summer (August–September in temperate Europe) — for detection of late-flowering species and assessment of the secondary growth community.
level2_equipment: 1m × 1m quadrat frame (folding); species identification field guide (national flora or regional grassland guide); Braun-Blanquet cover class reference card; GPS; field datasheet; camera. Optional: clinometer for sward height; vegetation height stick.
level2_reference: Braun-Blanquet J (1964) Pflanzensoziologie, 3rd edn. Springer, Vienna; Rodwell JS et al. (1992) British Plant Communities Vol. 3 — Grasslands and Montane Communities. Cambridge University Press [for NVC context].
level3_protocol_name: Full phytosociological survey — NVC or equivalent national vegetation classification
level3_output_metric: NVC community type or phytosociological association (primary classification output); mean Ellenberg N, R, F values per site; total species richness; forb:grass ratio; Sørensen similarity coefficient between survey years (beta diversity); soil pH, available N, available P.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring — late May to late June in temperate European systems. Required: 4+ weeks since last grazing or cutting event. Soil sampling at same visit.
level3_seasonal_secondary: None — single annual survey at primary window recommended for maximum comparability.
level3_equipment: 4m × 4m relevé frame; full national flora (e.g. Clive Stace, Flora of Great Britain and Ireland for UK context); NVC or equivalent community classification key; laptop with MATCH/TABLEFIT or equivalent; soil sampling equipment; laboratory access for soil chemistry.
level3_reference: Rodwell JS et al. (1992) British Plant Communities. Cambridge University Press; Ellenberg H et al. (1992) Zeigerwerte der Pflanzen in Mitteleuropa. Scripta Geobotanica 18. [geo]
primary_reference: Braun-Blanquet J (1964) Pflanzensoziologie, 3rd edn. Springer, Vienna; Rodwell JS et al. (1992) British Plant Communities Vol. 3 — Grasslands and Montane Communities. Cambridge University Press [for NVC context].

profile_name: Field margin and uncropped boundary vegetation
hard_prerequisite: A defined uncropped strip (grass margin, wildflower strip, buffer strip, or equivalent) of minimum 0.5m width adjacent to a field boundary. Not applicable where cultivation extends to the field edge with no margin.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — community composition survey
monitoring_stage: Fast–medium (Stage 2 from Year 1). Forb species richness in established margins
response_timescale: Forb species cover increase and first flowering plant appear
soil_type_associations: Sandy, free-draining soils — Support diverse forb communities where management is low-intensity — good drainage and lower nutrient status create conditions for forb-grass balance. Most productive monitoring context for detecting forb diversity in margins adjacent to arable fields on sandy soils.

Heavy clay soils — Lower forb diversity potential — clay soils favour coarse grasses (Arrhenatherum, Dactylis) under any management. Structural diversity (tussock formation, varied height) is a more achievable and monitoring-appropriate target than species richness on heavy clay margins.

Calcareous soils — Highest diversity potential under low-input management — calcicolous forbs (Centaurea scabiosa, Daucus carota, Medicago sativa, Origanum vulgare) establish readily in unpolluted calcareous margin soils. [geo]
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P14: Flowering forb cover detectable and measurably higher within the first season of delayed cutting (first cut deferred to July or later in temperate Europe [~] [geo]); species richness increase over 2–4 seasons as delayed cutting allows seed set and seed bank replenishment. P16: Vegetative cover establishment within 1 growing season of buffer strip seeding or natural establishment; forb species richness increasing from baseline (near zero) to monitoring-relevant levels within 2–4 seasons; structural diversity (tussock, varied height) developing within 3–5 years of undisturbed management. P11: Species richness increase detectable within 1–2 seasons of herbicide cessation; flowering forb presence confirms management change within one growing season; full community recovery from seed bank requires 3–7 years of consistent no-spray management.
level1_protocol_name: Field margin walkthrough — forb presence and structural diversity assessment
level1_output_metric: Non-grass plant species count per 50m section; sward height class; structural features present; management evidence record.
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–July in temperate Europe) — when forbs are in flower and most identifiable and sward height is at its management-determined maximum before the main cutting season. Agricultural management context: record date of last cut and whether herbicide has been applied.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — structural features (tussock, bare soil) most visible after summer vegetation senescence.
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; camera; regional wildflower guide (optional). No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Marshall EJP, Moonen AC (2002) — see References; Ecdysis 1000 Farms margin vegetation protocol.
level2_protocol_name: Field margin community composition survey — species-level quadrat assessment
level2_output_metric: Species richness per quadrat; forb:grass cover ratio; legume cover (%); sward structural diversity score (0–4); Ellenberg N mean [geo]; Shannon diversity index.
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — same as Level 1.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn.
level2_equipment: 0.5m × 1m quadrat frame; regional margin and grassland flora field guide; Braun-Blanquet cover reference; measuring stick (30cm and 60cm marks); GPS; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Full margin phytosociological survey with simultaneous pollinator and natural enemy assessment
level3_output_metric: NVC community type; full species richness; pollinator visit rate (visits per m of margin per minute); wild bee functional group diversity; ground beetle species richness and carabid functional group composition.
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring — simultaneous vegetation, pollinator, and pitfall survey.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: pitfall traps (5cm diameter, propylene glycol preservative); ground beetle identification key; pollinator species guide.
level3_reference: Pywell et al. (2005); Marshall & Moonen (2002) — see References.
primary_reference: Pywell et al. (2005); Marshall & Moonen (2002) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Hedgerow, woody boundary feature, shelter belt, or tree line present within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. This profile is not applicable in landscapes with no linear woody vegetation features — entirely open, boundary-free field systems.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — structured walkthrough variant
monitoring_stage: Slow (Stage 3, Year 5+) for full structural recovery. However, measurable struct
response_timescale: Canopy height increase and gap closure through existing wood
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P20: Canopy height increases measurably within 1–3 years of cutting frequency reduction; gap frequency declines within 3–5 years as woody growth closes existing gaps; vertical layer count increases within 5–7 years as shrub layer consolidates and tree layer emerges from retained standards. P19: Planted species establishment confirmed within 1 season; planted species contributing to canopy continuity within 5–10 years; species richness increase from planted stock detectable within 3–5 years as planted shrubs reach recognisable flowering form.
level1_protocol_name: Hedgerow structural condition walkthrough — community observer assessment
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer (May–June in temperate Europe) — before cutting, when foliage is fully open and canopy continuity is visually assessable. Agricultural management context: assess before annual cutting cycle begins, if possible. Avoid periods immediately after cutting.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Winter (December–February in temperate Europe) — leaf-off condition reveals structural gaps not visible in leaf. Records structural skeleton of the hedge without leaf cover. Complementary to spring survey.
level1_equipment: 50m tape measure; 2m ranging pole or stick (for height reference); field datasheet; camera; binoculars (optional, useful for canopy inspection without entering the hedge).
level1_reference: UK Countryside Survey hedgerow condition assessment methodology (Barr & Gillespie 2000); Hedgerow Survey Handbook (Defra 2007) [geo — UK]. Structural metrics applicable globally; species recording requires regional botanical resources.
level2_protocol_name: Hedgerow measured structural and botanical survey — quantitative assessment
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring (May–June in temperate European context) — full leaf canopy for canopy continuity assessment; most species identifiable. Before annual cutting if possible.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Winter — structural skeleton assessment; useful for gap identification without leaf cover.
level2_equipment: 30m tape measure; clinometer or electronic rangefinder (for canopy height); regional hedgerow flora field guide; field datasheet; GPS; camera. Optional: spherical densiometer for canopy cover confirmation.
level2_reference: Barr CJ, Gillespie MK (2000) Estimating hedgerow length and pattern characteristics in Great Britain using Countryside Survey data. Journal of Environmental Management 60:23–32. [geo — UK context; apply structural framework globally]
level3_protocol_name: Full hedgerow phytosociological survey with landscape connectivity assessment
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring — same as Level 2. Landscape GIS mapping can be conducted any season using aerial imagery.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: GPS with sub-metre accuracy; GIS software (QGIS or equivalent); access to high-resolution aerial imagery; NVC or regional vegetation classification keys; full regional flora.
level3_reference: Defra (2007) The Hedgerow Survey Handbook. Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, London [geo]; Barr & Gillespie (2000); Hinsley SA, Bellamy PE (2000) — see References.
primary_reference: Barr CJ, Gillespie MK (2000) Estimating hedgerow length and pattern characteristics in Great Britain using Countryside Survey data. Journal of Environmental Management 60:23–32. [geo — UK context; apply structural framework globally]

hard_prerequisite: Permanent or seasonal water body, stream, river, ditch, pond, or wetland feature present within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. This profile is not applicable in landscapes with no surface water features.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — community composition survey at fixed monitoring points along water margin
response_timescale: Riparian buffer zone vegetation shifts — notably decline of
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P16: Nitrophilous species cover declines measurably within 1–2 seasons of buffer establishment and fencing; forb and sedge diversity increases within 2–4 seasons; riparian buffer canopy cover increases within 5–10 years of tree/shrub planting establishment. P05: Submerged plant species richness increases progressively within 2–5 years of measurable water nitrate decline (confirmed by A17 abiotic precursor); Ranunculus and Callitriche presence at monitoring points is the most interpretable species-level positive indicator. P13: Riparian buffer zone vegetation cover increases within 1–2 seasons of livestock exclusion; bank erosion features (visible undercutting) stabilise within 2–4 seasons; canopy continuity (if riparian trees present) increases measurably within 3–5 years.
level1_protocol_name: Aquatic vegetation zone presence walkthrough — community observer assessment
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — when aquatic and riparian vegetation is in active growth and all four zones are maximally expressed. Agricultural management context: assess before any ditch cutting or bank management operations. Avoid surveying within 3 weeks of ditch cleaning.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer — canopy cover assessment is most meaningful in summer when maximum riparian canopy is present.
level1_equipment: 50m tape measure; field datasheet; camera; walking shoes suitable for soft ground. No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Simplified from the Ecdysis 1000 Farms water margin vegetation assessment protocol; LDSF riparian vegetation assessment framework.
level2_protocol_name: Aquatic and riparian vegetation community composition survey — fixed quadrat and transect method
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — when aquatic plant communities are fully developed and most species identifiable. Before ditch cleaning or cutting. Record date relative to most recent management event.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late summer — useful for submerged plant assessment when water is clearest (before autumn leaf fall turbidity) and for canopy cover confirmation.
level2_equipment: 1m × 1m quadrat frame; survey rake or telescopic pole (for submerged zone); spherical densiometer (convex, for canopy cover); regional aquatic flora field guide; field datasheet; GPS; camera; waterproof notebook; waders if needed for emergent zone access.
level3_protocol_name: Full aquatic phytosociological survey with network-scale riparian canopy assessment
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring — same as Level 2.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: Lugol's iodine solution; phase contrast microscope for diatom analysis; GIS software and access to high-resolution aerial imagery for network-scale canopy analysis.
level3_reference: Baattrup-Pedersen & Riis (1999); Moss B (2010); Ellenberg H et al. (1992) [geo]; Lorenz AW et al. (2016) — see References.
primary_reference: Baattrup-Pedersen & Riis (1999); Moss B (2010); Ellenberg H et al. (1992) [geo]; Lorenz AW et al. (2016) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — but the expected monitoring outcome in regularly cultivated soils is confirmed absence of biological crust (which is itself informative as a management intensity indicator). Monitoring both presence and absence is ecologically meaningful.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — community composition survey (fixed quadrat, surface cover assessment)
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1 in semi-arid/no-till; medium in temperate grassland co
response_timescale: Cyanobacterial and algal crust colonisation of bare stable s
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P01: Cyanobacterial and algal crust detectable (darkened cohesive soil surface in inter-row spaces) within 1–3 seasons of no-till adoption in semi-arid and dryland contexts; lichen crust development within 5–10 years of stable no-disturbance conditions; bryophyte crust in temperate no-till contexts within 3–7 years.
level1_protocol_name: Biological soil crust presence and cover assessment — hand lens visual survey
level1_output_metric: BSC functional types present (list); estimated cover % per functional type; crust integrity class; soil moisture condition at observation.
level1_seasonal_primary: Spring — when soil moisture levels support visible crust activity and biological pigmentation is maximally expressed. Avoid extremely dry conditions when crust contracts and darkens to a point of difficult visibility (if gentle moistening with a drop of water restores colour, crust is present but dormant). Agricultural management context: assess at least 4 weeks after any cultivation or intensive livestock trampling event.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — after first rains activate dormant crusts; biologically active crust is most visible.
level1_equipment: Hand lens (10×); 20cm × 20cm metal or card frame; field datasheet; camera with macro capability.
level1_reference: Belnap J, Lange OL (eds) (2001) Biological Soil Crusts: Structure, Function, and Management — see References; Ecdysis 1000 Farms BSC assessment adapted protocol.
level2_protocol_name: Point-intercept BSC cover measurement with functional type classification
level2_output_metric: BSC cover % per functional type (from point-intercept); chlorophyll a concentration (µg/cm2); penetration resistance (kPa) of crust vs bare soil; crust integrity score.
level2_seasonal_primary: Spring — same as Level 1. Assess immediately after rainfall event for maximum biological activity.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn.
level2_equipment: Point-intercept frame (100-point grid over 1m2); fine pin or knitting needle; 1cm2 sampling template; methanol (5ml per sample); spectrophotometer or colorimeter; pocket penetrometer (optional); field datasheet; camera with macro capability.
level3_protocol_name: Species-level BSC community characterisation with nitrogen fixation measurement
level3_output_metric: Cyanobacterial species richness; diversity indices; N-fixation rate (nmol C2H4/cm2/hour; converted to kg N/ha/year using site-specific scaling); community composition similarity between sites and years.
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring at peak biological activity (moist conditions). N-fixation assay within 24 hours of sample collection.
level3_equipment: Compound microscope (400×); cyanobacterial identification keys; gas chromatograph for acetylene reduction; gas-tight containers; acetylene gas supply; 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing access.
level3_reference: Belnap J et al. (2001); Eldridge DJ, Greene RSB (1994) — see References.
primary_reference: Belnap J et al. (2001); Eldridge DJ, Greene RSB (1994) — see References.

profile_name: Scattered trees and parkland tree cover
hard_prerequisite: At least one tree (individual woody plant >3m tall with defined crown) present within the monitored landscape. Not applicable in treeless arable landscapes with no scattered trees, although in such landscapes this profile would typically generate a recommendation for tree planting under Practice P19.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — cover and census assessment (individual tree census with crown cover measurement)
monitoring_stage: Slow (Stage 3, Year 5+). Individual tree condition changes are detectable within
response_timescale: Crown condition deterioration or improvement is detectable w
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P10: Crown cover % increase measurable within 5–10 years of tree establishment in agroforestry systems; individual tree crown diameter increase of 0.3–0.8m per year [~] is the expected growth rate in temperate silvo-pastoral conditions. P19: Planted tree survival rate (% of planted individuals alive and in growth) confirmed within 1–3 seasons; crown cover contribution from planted stock measurable within 5–10 years; veteran tree feature development requires decades — not a short-term verification metric.
level1_protocol_name: Individual tree census — visual crown assessment and cover estimation
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — when full leaf cover makes crown assessment most meaningful and crown cover estimation most accurate. Species identification also easier in leaf.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Winter — useful for crown structure assessment without leaf cover; dead wood and decay features more visible; veteran features more accessible for assessment.
level1_equipment: GPS receiver (smartphone is adequate); permanent tree labels or marking paint; field datasheet; 10m tape or range pole (for regeneration radius); camera. No specialist equipment.
level1_reference: Manning AD, Fischer J, Lindenmayer DB (2006) scattered trees census methodology — see References; Ecdysis 1000 Farms scattered tree protocol.
level2_protocol_name: Measured individual tree census with densiometer crown cover assessment
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring — as per Level 1.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Winter — dead wood assessment more accessible.
level2_equipment: 30m tape measure; girth tape; spherical densiometer; GPS (sub-metre preferred); permanent tree labels; field datasheet; camera with GPS tagging.
level2_reference: BS 5837:2012 Trees in relation to design, demolition and construction — arboricultural assessment methodology [geo — UK]; Manning et al. (2006) census methodology.
level3_protocol_name: Full arboricultural and ecological assessment with remote sensing canopy mapping
level3_seasonal_primary: Spring/early summer for beetle trapping and full leaf crown assessment. Increment coring any season but spring preferred.
level3_equipment: Swedish increment borer and core handling equipment; LiDAR scanner or photogrammetric drone; flight intercept traps; 70% ethanol for beetle preservation; compound microscope and beetle identification keys.
level3_reference: Manning et al. (2006); Lindenmayer et al. (2012); Gibbons & Lindenmayer (2002); Nair (1993) — see References.
primary_reference: BS 5837:2012 Trees in relation to design, demolition and construction — arboricultural assessment methodology [geo — UK]; Manning et al. (2006) census methodology.

hard_prerequisite: None — the profile is applicable both where regeneration is present (to track trajectory) and where it is absent (to confirm suppression by management or grazing pressure). In landscapes with no seed source within dispersal distance and no planting programme, the expected outcome is confirmed absence.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — presence and spread mapping (area-based cover by height class)
response_timescale: Change in <50cm height class density detectable within 1–2 s
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P19: Planted individuals surviving in <50cm class within 1 season; 50cm–2m class within 2–4 years for fast-growing species; >2m class within 5–10 years. Natural regeneration from planted seed sources appearing within 3–7 years as planted individuals reach reproductive maturity. P20: Natural regeneration in margin zones adjacent to managed hedgerows appears within 1–3 seasons of reduced cutting and livestock exclusion from the margin zone; species diversity of regeneration increases over 3–7 years as different hedgerow species reach reproductive maturity and disperse seed. P13: <50cm height class density detectable within 1–2 seasons of grazing exclusion or significant pressure reduction; 50cm–2m class appears within 2–5 years where palatable species predominate; height class diversity (all three classes present) requires 5–10 years of sustained management change.
level1_protocol_name: Woody regeneration presence and spread mapping — height class cover estimation
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — when woody regeneration is in active growth and all height classes are maximally expressed. Agricultural management context: assess 4–6 weeks after last cutting or grazing event to allow recovery assessment.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — before leaf drop; browse damage on <50cm class visible; planted stock survival assessable.
level1_equipment: 50m tape measure; GPS; ranging pole (1m and 2m marked) for height class assignment; field datasheet; camera.
level1_reference: Ecdysis 1000 Farms woody regeneration monitoring protocol; Bullock JM et al. (2011) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Point-intercept transect with species-level regeneration census
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring — when leaves are fully expanded and seedlings most identifiable. Record date relative to last management event.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — before leaf drop; survival assessment.
level2_equipment: 50m tape measure; point-intercept pole (1m or 2m rigid pole); GPS; regional field guide for woody plants (seedling and sapling identification); field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: GPS-mapped full regeneration census with connectivity and provenance analysis
level3_seasonal_primary: Late spring for census and leaf tissue sampling.
level3_equipment: GPS (sub-metre); GIS and remote sensing access; leaf press and silica gel for tissue sampling; laboratory access for microsatellite analysis.
level3_reference: Bullock JM et al. (2011); Pakeman RJ, Nolan AM (2009) — see References.
primary_reference: Bullock JM et al. (2011); Pakeman RJ, Nolan AM (2009) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: None — the profile is applicable both in the presence of invasive species (area-based spread mapping) and in their confirmed absence (monitoring for early detection). Absence confirmed by structured survey is itself a meaningful monitoring outcome.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — presence and spread mapping
monitoring_stage: Fast (Stage 2 from Year 1). Invasive species cover and occurrence changes are de
response_timescale: Population reduction following effective mechanical or chemi
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P29: Invasive cover declines measurably within 1–2 seasons of effective control (method-dependent); populations suppressed below competitive exclusion threshold within 2–5 years of sustained management; long-term monitoring is essential — many invasive species require multi-year sustained management for durable population reduction.
level1_protocol_name: Invasive species presence and area mapping — community observer survey
level1_output_metric: Invasive species occurrence count (number of GPS-marked occurrences per species); total estimated invaded area (m2) per species; trajectory per occurrence (new / expanding / stable / declining / absent); spread rate (change in total invaded area between surveys).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — peak growth when most invasive species are most identifiable and cover is maximally expressed. For giant hogweed: assess in May–June [geo] when plants are recognisable by leaf shape before they become dangerously tall. For Reynoutria: assess in July–August when shoots are fully developed.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Early autumn — for detecting late-season invasive species (Conyza, some Fallopia in seed) and assessing management outcome from summer control operations.
level1_equipment: Regional invasive species photographic reference card (produced by deployment team); GPS receiver (smartphone adequate); field datasheet; camera.
level1_reference: Simberloff D et al. (2013) Impacts of biological invasions — see References; regional invasive species control guidance from national or regional invasive species authorities.
level2_protocol_name: Quantitative invasive species cover assessment with GPS patch mapping
level2_output_metric: Invasive cover % per species per monitoring zone (transect-derived); total invaded area (m2) per species (GPS patch area); annual spread rate (m2/year); invasion front extent (linear metres of monitoring zone invaded).
level2_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early summer — peak growth for most target invasive species.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Autumn — spread rate assessment; management outcome evaluation.
level2_equipment: GPS (sub-metre preferred for patch boundary mapping); species-level invasive identification guide (regional); transect tape; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Remote sensing patch mapping with population genetics invasion pathway analysis
level3_output_metric: Total invaded area (ha) across the landscape unit (remote sensing derived); genetic diversity per patch (number of genets vs clonal individuals); source population identification; propagule pressure index.
level3_equipment: Multispectral drone or satellite imagery access; spectral analysis software; tissue sampling and genotyping laboratory access.
level3_reference: Simberloff et al. (2013); Pyšek & Richardson (2010) — see References.
primary_reference: Simberloff et al. (2013); Pyšek & Richardson (2010) — see References.

profile_name: Permanent crop and orchard understory vegetation
hard_prerequisite: Permanent crop system with inter-row or under-canopy space available for understory community development. Not applicable in systems with fully sealed soil surfaces (concrete or gravel inter-rows throughout) — though even a 0.5m grass strip at the row edge qualifies for monitoring.
monitoring_task_type: Biological community composition — community composition survey (inter-row quadrat assessment)
response_timescale: Forb cover increase and first flowering plant appearance in
b2_expected_direction_of_change: P21: Forb cover detectable within 1–2 seasons of nitrogen input cessation; species richness increases progressively over 2–5 years where seed bank is intact; legume cover (clover, vetch) typically appears within 1–3 seasons as nitrogen-competitive advantage of grasses diminishes. P14: Flowering forb cover in inter-row strip detectable and measurably higher within the first season of delayed mowing; number of flowering species at crop bloom timing increases within 1–2 seasons; pollinator visits to understory flowers increase proportionally. P11: Vegetation cover in inter-row increases from baseline (near zero or sparse) to measurable cover within 1 growing season of herbicide cessation; first forb species appear within 1–2 seasons; species richness reaches monitoring threshold within 2–5 years depending on seed bank condition.
level1_protocol_name: Inter-row understory walkthrough — functional group and canopy cover assessment
level1_seasonal_primary: At crop bloom time — when pollination is occurring and the inter-row understory is most critically needed for wild pollinator support. Agricultural management context: this is the critical survey timing — the ecological information most relevant to the pollination service. A secondary survey at peak summer (July–August in temperate Europe) records the sustained season foraging resource.
level1_seasonal_secondary: Late summer — secondary flowering season resource assessment.
level1_equipment: 50m tape measure; field datasheet; camera. No specialist equipment required.
level1_reference: Ecdysis 1000 Farms orchard understory protocol; Tschumi et al. (2015) inter-row strip design reference — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Inter-row understory community composition survey — species-level quadrat assessment with densiometer canopy cover
level2_seasonal_primary: At crop bloom timing — same as Level 1 primary. This is the most ecologically critical survey window.
level2_seasonal_secondary: Late summer — for secondary flowering season assessment.
level2_equipment: 1m × 0.5m quadrat frame; spherical densiometer (convex); regional field guide for orchard, vineyard, and inter-row flora; Braun-Blanquet cover class reference card; GPS; field datasheet; camera.
level3_protocol_name: Full inter-row phytosociological survey with simultaneous pollinator transect — integrated understory-service assessment
level3_seasonal_primary: At crop bloom — for both understory survey and pollinator visit count. This is the only window when the service delivery connection between understory forbs and crop pollination is directly measurable.
level3_equipment: As Level 2 plus: pollinator identification reference (regional bee and hoverfly guide); tally counter; stopwatch; hand lens for bee identification.
level3_reference: Brown & Paxton (2009); Tschumi et al. (2015); Wyss (1995); Aguilar et al. (2006) — see References.
primary_reference: Brown & Paxton (2009); Tschumi et al. (2015); Wyss (1995); Aguilar et al. (2006) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Confirmed termite presence within or adjacent to the monitored landscape. Not applicable in cool temperate biomes.
response_timescale: Fungus-grower and grass-harvester colony density increases m
level1_protocol_name: Termite mound count transect and activity assessment
level1_output_metric: Active mound density (mounds per hectare per mound type); activity score per mound; proportion of mounds with active colonies.
level1_seasonal_primary: Wet season peak or immediately post-rainy season — when colony activity is highest and foraging galleries are most visible.
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; tape measure; camera; sturdy boot for gentle surface tap to confirm colony activity (colony responds to vibration with worker and soldier emergence).
level1_reference: Eggleton P et al. (2002) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Transect mound count with pitfall traps and soil core extraction for functional group assessment
level2_output_metric: Termite species/morphospecies richness; functional group proportions; biomass estimate per group; colony density from core samples.
level2_equipment: Pitfall traps; soil coring equipment; regional termite identification key (soldier head morphology); dissecting microscope (10–20×).
level2_reference: Eggleton et al. (2002); Jouquet et al. (2011) — see References.
level3_protocol_name: Full termite community assessment with molecular species identification and service quantification
level3_output_metric: Full species list; functional group ecosystem service rates (infiltration improvement factor; N-fixation rate kg/ha/year).
level3_reference: Eggleton et al. (2002); Jouquet et al. (2011) — see References.
primary_reference: Eggleton et al. (2002); Jouquet et al. (2011) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Permanent water body (pond, river, irrigation canal, rice paddy system, or equivalent) with confirmed or likely turtle presence. Water body must retain water year-round or through a defined seasonal wet period adequate for turtle survival.
primary_monitoring_role: Water body quality and permanence indicator
response_timescale: Basking count index increasing at improved water bodies foll
level1_protocol_name: Freshwater turtle basking count survey — community observer population index
level1_output_metric: Maximum basking count per survey; size class proportions; species list; basking site occupancy ratio (turtles/available basking sites).
level1_seasonal_primary: Warm season — when turtles are most active and basking most frequently. In tropical contexts: dry season mornings when water levels are lower and basking sites more exposed. In subtropical contexts: spring–autumn warm period.
level1_equipment: Binoculars (10×); field datasheet; camera with zoom lens; photographic turtle identification guide (regional).
level1_reference: van Dijk PP et al. (2014) — see References.
level2_protocol_name: Hoop trap survey — quantitative turtle abundance and population structure assessment
level2_output_metric: Capture per unit effort (CPUE); size class proportions; male:female ratio; species composition.
level2_seasonal_primary: Warm season when turtles are most active.
level2_equipment: Floating hoop traps with float collar (critical welfare requirement); sardine bait; measuring tape; field datasheet; camera; waterproof boots.
level3_protocol_name: Mark-recapture population estimation with PIT tagging and eDNA
level3_output_metric: Population size estimate; survival probability; eDNA concentration (copies/L); nest site count and success rate.
level3_reference: van Dijk et al. (2014); Sievert & Gutzke (1993) — see References.
primary_reference: Sievert & Gutzke (1993) — see References.

hard_prerequisite: Permanent or seasonal pond, ditch, or slow-flowing watercourse with confirmed or likely newt presence. Not applicable where no suitable breeding water body exists within 2km of the monitoring area.
level1_protocol_name: Torch survey and bottle trap survey (licence required for great crested newt)
level1_output_metric: Smooth newt count per torch survey (individuals per metre of bank scanned); GCN presence/absence from eDNA; water body habitat quality score (fish present Y/N; pH adequate Y/N; bank vegetation Y/N).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring (March–May in temperate Europe [geo]) — peak breeding season; maximum newt visibility in shallow water during torch surveys. eDNA detection window: April–June (optimal); outside this window, eDNA concentration in water falls below reliable detection threshold.
level1_equipment: Powerful waterproof torch (minimum 300 lumens); field datasheet; for eDNA: sterile 1L bottles, 0.45μm filter membranes, ethanol — as Profile 21 Level 2 eDNA protocol.

conditionality_criteria: Activate in deployment contexts where large soil invertebrate communities — particularly tropical and subtropical systems with diverse scarab beetles, centipedes, large millipedes, and carabid larvae — are ecologically significant as soil engineers and prey base. Also applicable in temperate systems where Profile 06 (Earthworms) alone is insufficient to characterise the full large soil invertebrate community. This profile supplements Profile 06 rather than replacing it.
hard_prerequisite: Active large soil invertebrate community (individuals >2cm body length) present and ecologically significant in the monitored soil profile. Not applicable where soil fauna is adequately characterised by Profile 06 (Earthworms) alone — i.e. most temperate European contexts where earthworms dominate the large soil invertebrate biomass.
level1_protocol_name: Visual hand-sorting of soil quadrats — the same method as Profile 06 (Earthworms), Level 2 hand-sorting, extended to record all invertebrates >2cm body length
level1_output_metric: Count per functional group per 900cm2 (30×30cm quadrat); biomass estimate (wet weight per functional group where scales available); functional group diversity index; presence of any pest species (scarab chafer larvae — record separately for Profile 41 cross-reference).
level1_seasonal_primary: Late spring to early autumn — when soil temperature is adequate for invertebrate activity (soil temperature >10°C [~]). Avoid very dry periods when invertebrates move deep into the soil profile below 20cm sampling depth.
level1_equipment: White sorting tray; forceps; waterproof gloves (centipedes of the genus Scolopendra can deliver a painful venomous bite — wear gloves); field datasheet; camera.

conditionality_criteria: Activate in deployment contexts where economic crop pest invertebrate monitoring provides information that directly guides integrated pest management (IPM) decisions within the LAHMP programme — specifically where the decision to apply pesticides, or not, depends on field-level pest population data that existing profiles do not provide. This profile is activated when pest pressure from specific invertebrate groups represents a primary management concern at the monitored farm. Do not activate where general pest pressure is adequately captured as secondary outputs from existing universal profiles (e.g. Profile 07 slugs, Profile 03 nematodes).
hard_prerequisite: Specific crop pest invertebrate community present and causing or threatening measurable crop damage at the monitoring site. Pest group identity must be specified at programme setup — the profile is pest-group specific, not a generic pest monitoring profile. Minimum specification: pest group (e.g. aphids / wireworms / cabbage root fly / whitefly / thrips); crop context; economic damage threshold reference.
level1_protocol_name: Visual crop inspection for pest species count and damage assessment against economic threshold
level1_output_metric: Pest density per plant (individuals or % damage); economic threshold status (below / at / above); natural enemy presence per plant (Y/N per group); parasitism rate where mummies visible (% parasitised aphids); ratio of pest density to natural enemy presence as the primary integrated pest management indicator.
level1_seasonal_primary: During active crop growth — timing depends entirely on the specific pest group and crop. Aphid monitoring: from crop emergence through flowering. Soil pest monitoring: at drilling and post-emergence. Leaf pest monitoring: during vegetative growth. Seasonal timing must be confirmed from the national IPM calendar for the pest group and crop in the deployment region.
level1_equipment: Field datasheet; hand lens (10×); regional crop pest identification guide; economic threshold reference table (from national IPM advisory service — e.g. AHDB Pest Bulletin for UK [geo]); camera.
